output: Mr President, Madam Vice-President, the Commission gives the impression of acting like a distracted student who is rushing to catch up before handing in his paper.
Certainly, the social package exists, but it is incomplete and can only be considered as a step towards the Commission taking social issues into account.

The President of the Commission has not taken on board the warning given by citizens in France, the Netherlands and Ireland, which would unfortunately have been the same elsewhere in the event of a popular vote.
These votes are wake-up calls.
If Europeans want more Europe, it is to build a social project and not simply to adopt more technical directives.
Regulations on the internal market are certainly necessary, but they are far from being the most important thing.
The 2009 programme, like previous ones, will miss the point.
Citizens do not expect the Commission to limit itself to completing or even simplifying legislation.
They expect the Commission to rise to the task assigned to it by the Treaties: to be the locomotive, the idea factory of the Union.
It is not enough for its President to be wise and silent, on guard behind the President of the Council.
We expect the Commission to act as a key player in the reflection on the nature of the kind of society we need.
But what is the social project in this programme?
The 2009 programme does not contain any serious errors, but it is not the programme our fellow citizens need.
Mr President, it will be up to the new College to take its responsibilities.
I only hope that it will be less deaf to the calls of peoples than the current one.
Mr President, first of all, a question for the Council.

What is the status of the high-level conference on Georgia, which had been proposed by the French Presidency, and how does the current Presidency of the Union intend to defend the territorial integrity of Georgia, while a country recognises two separatist regions, Abkhazia and South Ossetia?
It is a pity that Mr Barroso is not here, because in his speech he was not convincing, perhaps because he himself is not convinced by the proposals that he has made to us.
I think that the financial and economic situation is much more serious than he has described.
Even today, I believe that those who follow the Internet can see very clearly that the stock markets continue to collapse despite the interventions.

So, for me, it's really the collapse of the current system, a system that is completely obsolete, the system that has led to bankruptcy, that has led to a planet being totally exploited and suffocated by greenhouse gas emissions, that has led to underdevelopment in the South and that has led to underdevelopment in our cities.
Madam President, Madam Commissioner, the current financial crisis clearly shows that when the problem is global, we need global action.
And this is especially true in the field of agriculture or in the food field.
That is why we created the World Trade Organization, on which we agree, which is trying to have global rules.

It turns out that it doesn't work quite as we would like, because we are faced with the following problem: we have to reconcile two things: free trade, which is necessary, and on which everyone agrees, but also protection, which is just as necessary.
And protecting national economies or agriculture does not mean protectionism.
To do this, the path we have taken for 60 years, since GATT, is an unbalanced path, which goes from the reduction of customs duties, with the objective of their abolition.
It so happens that technically, the reduction of duties
It's difficult. The proof: a whole series of mathematical formulas, the South Korean formula, the European formula, or the most well-known formula, the Swiss formula.
It doesn't really work because a product is not unitary.
It is made up of many elements, with very different rules of origin, and technically reducing customs duties is not as simple as one might think.
We are therefore in a technical deadlock, and we are trying politically, through negotiations, to get out of this deadlock.

Now, it so happens that technologically, scientists have invented a new customs technology: the technology of deductible customs duties, deductible by the exporter from the importing country's economy.
Specifically, this deductible customs duty becomes a customs credit equal to the amount of customs duty that the exporter has paid in the importing country.
This customs credit has three characteristics: it is repayable, negotiable and transferable.
It is refundable at the time when the exporter goes to buy from the importer, he can deduct the customs duty he has borne.
It is negotiable because if the exporting company that has paid 100 in duties has nothing to buy from the importer, it negotiates its customs credit on the stock market or at the bank.
And it is Bonifield if we want to help developing countries, the importer to offer an amount of customs credit higher than the amount of customs duties.
Well, with this technique, we create an international trade currency whose money supply is equal to the amount of existing customs duties.
For example, the European Union would offer EUR 13 billion in international currency in the commercial sector.
Mr President, the Russian-Georgian conflict and the financial crisis have given the European Union the opportunity to assert itself on the world stage as a full-fledged, first-rate political player.
Thanks to your determination, Mr President of the Council, Europe has managed to unite and find coordinated, effective and rapid responses.
In the face of crises, Europe proves that it exists and demonstrates its added value.
In the matter of immigration, for example, the "every man for himself" approach was no longer in fashion.
The adoption by the European Council of the Pact on Migration and Asylum, initiated by the French Presidency, constitutes a considerable step forward.
On climate change, Europe must set itself concrete objectives and show the way to its global partners.

You have already expressed yourself on this subject, Mr President of the Council, but we would like this agreement to be as balanced as possible, because it must take into account the economic situation of our countries, which is currently very destabilised.
but also to ask ourselves what kind of development we will choose for the future.
In order to meet the challenges ahead, the European Union more than ever needs the Lisbon Treaty.
We understand the concerns of our Irish friends, but the status quo is not an option.
Ireland must propose a solution at the European Council in December, and many of us are calling for this.
Over the past few weeks, we have proudly observed a strong political Europe, united in adversity, determined in its dealings with its partners and listened to on the international stage.
We hope that this new European mindset becomes the norm and endures.
The Lisbon Treaty is the best solution we have at our disposal to achieve this.
Madam President, Minister, I was in Tbilisi when President Sarkozy came to negotiate the peace plan, and of course I pay tribute to him for having acted quickly and succeeded in stopping this war.
But this war is a bit of a failure for us. We have been timid for 14 years. We have silently watched the escalation of provocations in the separatist regions.

This war has certainly woken Europe up by placing it in front of its responsibilities, but the fire is still smouldering in the Caucasus and we must do everything we can to definitively settle the frozen conflicts for the security of the whole of Europe.
I am also aware of the divisions, Minister, among European countries on Georgia ’ s entry into NATO, and personally I am opposed to it.
I have a proposal for you: I will ask the European Union to propose a neutral status for these countries in the Caucasus.
Only a neutral status will calm tensions with Russia and definitively prevent this sub-region from experiencing new conflicts.
This status will ensure the security of these young democracies and contribute to our own security.

Madam President, Commissioner, Minister, over the past three days, the European Development Days have taken place, during which we have discussed at length the food crisis that is seriously affecting the poorest countries, and
All the speakers stressed the absolute necessity for developing countries to develop their own agriculture, using their land and natural resources in the best possible way.

By the way, the lyrical flights of those who yesterday and today still called for reducing the state's intervention, public spending, liberalisation in all directions, privatisation and relegating agriculture to a secondary role,

activity, almost prehistoric. This about-face, therefore, in favour of massive public investment in agricultural production and food sovereignty, all this has something at once absurd and scandalous about it.
The future of agriculture in developing countries is directly linked to the debate we are having today.

Unfortunately, I do not feel that this health check of the CAP, initiated before the peak of the food riots and the market chaos, takes into account the situation of southern peasantry and draws all the consequences of the serious crises we are experiencing today: ecological, social, economic and financial.

I therefore deeply regret that the strong relationship between agriculture in the North and in the South is so little addressed, both in the Commission's proposals and in Parliament's report, and that the withdrawal of the promised billion euros shows how long the road still is.
Mr President, the report we will vote on tomorrow marks the culmination of a year of work and negotiations devoted to the future of the common agricultural policy and of our farmers.
Indeed, it was on 20 November that Mrs Fischer Boel came to make her proposals to us on the state of health.
Following Mr Goebbels ’ interim report, we now have Mr Capoulas Santos ’ report on the Commission proposal.
We should congratulate our two colleagues on their excellent work.
The European Parliament's Committee on Agriculture and Rural Development has stood firm in the face of the Commission's initial proposals, which we judged to be far too liberal.
I will remain vigilant with regard to the milk sector and, in particular, the measures preceding the abolition of quotas.
I am thinking in particular of mountain milk producers, who must not be the poor relations of this reform, and we must take care not to leave them behind, as they will need more support than others.
I do not want to play Cassandra, but how can we imagine the French dairy industry grouped together in one and the same region?
I therefore welcome the proposal to set up a milk fund and the proposal to assess the increase in milk quotas as from 2010, depending on market developments.

The progressive modulation calculation made by the Committee on Agriculture seems fair to me and would allow us to strengthen development in rural areas, and to preserve biodiversity in a sustainable way.
I also welcome the clarification of eco-conditionality, which until now has been a real headache for farmers.

I also wish that we maintain the level of the tobacco fund, which allows us to maintain our small farms in Europe, as well as a non-negligible workforce in a region where nothing else is exploitable, and which allows us to avoid imported products.
Finally, Minister, we have greatly appreciated your commitment throughout these negotiations.
You have shown a lot of patience and a lot of determination, and you have not counted your time, far from it.
It was a real pleasure to work alongside you.
You have been very effective with us.
I know that we can count on you.
Mr President, thank you for the work done and for the conclusions and orientations proposed by the health check of the CAP, by Mr Capoulas Santos ’ s report.
Agriculture is indeed an economic sector that cannot respond to the simple signals of the market.
Its activity has an impact, of course, on food self-sufficiency, but also, and we forget it too often, on territories and on employment.
The abolition of market instruments would be a serious blow to our agriculture.
The decoupling, for example, is a real difficulty for certain livestock areas, dairy cows and sheep, and I was pleased to hear Mrs Fischer Boel earlier on this issue.
Moreover, it is clear that strict adherence to historical references would place a huge brake on any possibility of reform.

However, I still believe that the question of the second pillar of the CAP is an essential one, because rural areas need to rely on agricultural activity in order to develop employment in rural areas.
We must therefore think about this second pillar, without, of course, cutting off the first.
Given that Mr Jouyet is leaving us, I think that Parliament should thank him
He was one of the most diligent ministers we have ever known.
I wish him good luck.

Madam President, I voted for the Perrin report on the integration of the OCM wine into the common organisation of the market, but I must say I did so with a heavy heart, because I consider that this common organisation of the market is not a simplification and does not increase transparency.
It will make life more complicated for viticulturists and the entire vitivinicultural sector.
Last night, the Commissioner tried to reassure us.
I hope that the Commission will keep its word and that, above all, the profession will continue to be adequately represented on the advisory committee, as has been the case since the first OCM.
Monsieur
Mr President, Minister, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, our group enthusiastically supports the Small Business Act that the Commission presented to us in June.

Certainly, as early as the 2000s, initiatives were taken in favour of SMEs, and I am thinking in particular of the excellent work by our colleague Dominique Vlasto on the SME charter. These initiatives were appreciated, but judged insufficient, because they remained too often at the level of good intentions.
Today, we must go further.
The serious economic crisis that our countries are going through, and whose consequences are particularly worrying for SMEs, urges us to do so, if need be.
In this respect, the Small Business Act is part of a new, resolutely proactive approach.
In a few weeks, we will be examining Mrs Merz's report on the Commission's communication, but today, by tabling this oral question, we wanted to address the Council.

Mr President, Minister, we took note with interest of the conclusions adopted yesterday by the Competitiveness Council, and we would like to know whether the European Council on 11 and 12 December will
will take up these conclusions and give a strong impetus to ensure that the main guiding principle, 'think small first', is quickly transposed into the regulatory framework of the Member States.
We would like the Council to commit itself to the principle of the action plan annexed, together with concrete measures.
In this respect, and in the short time available to me, I would like to stress three areas in which we need binding initiatives.
impact studies, which should be systematic, and regular monitoring, with the European Parliament controlling the state of implementation of the Small Business Act in the Member States.
Access to public procurement.
The code of conduct has no binding force; we must be more imaginative and more inventive and propose concrete, appropriate measures that will really open up these markets to SMEs.
Finally, access to funding, more essential than ever in the current context,
Your proposals are good, Minister, but they must also include support for supply.
There is urgency.
Thank you for assuring us of the Council ’ s willingness.
Mr President, Minister, Commissioner, we all know that SMEs are the backbone of the European Union, since they employ more than 70% of the EU workforce and represent 99% of European enterprises.

As has already been said, these millions of small and medium-sized enterprises are now worried and threatened by the financial crisis, because it is reducing consumers' purchasing power and confidence and affecting their order books.

It is therefore the cash flow of SMEs that is at risk, and we must therefore ensure that their activities are supported by making sure that banks guarantee effective access to the funding needed to ensure the continuity of their operations.
That is the whole point of the conclusions of the Competitiveness Council of 1 December, which the Minister mentioned, and of the recovery plan approved by the European finance ministers.
Making the needs of SMEs a permanent political priority for the Union is essential, and I would like to welcome the action plan in favour of the Small Business Act for Europe.
It reflects the desire to ensure the effective application of the guiding principle: give priority to the small first, that is to say, to European SMEs.
However, as the Commissioner said, and as we all say, the Council must really commit itself with the other institutions
European Union. In order for this principle to be made effective now, we must go beyond the declaration and move on to implementation in all European policies and programmes.
However, we can say that this action plan is a real response to the urgency of the situation.
And I would like to thank you, Minister, for the French Presidency ’ s commitment, which has enabled us to translate intentions into action quickly.

Mr President, Madam President-in-Office of the Council, Commissioner, I fully support the rapporteur, who is defending the position that was ours at first reading, a position rejected by the governments of the Member States.
It was urgent to bring our legislation on on-call time into line with the case law of the Court of Justice. That is now done.
The Cercas report provides balanced and protective answers for workers.
All standby time is counted as working time.
The compensatory rest period must take place immediately after the period of service.
This is a matter of common sense, in order to guarantee decent working conditions, particularly for medical professions.
But the reform of the Working Time Directive also gives us the opportunity to advance our European social law by scrapping individual opt-outs.
The Cercas report seizes this opportunity and proposes a progressive elimination of any possibility of derogating from the maximum legal working week.
We need to look reality in the face.
It is a joke to say that the employee is in a situation of equality with his employer and can refuse what is offered to him.
Ladies and gentlemen, we must clearly show the governments of the Member States that the text they wish to impose on us is unacceptable.

And amidst the chorus of praise that will certainly be showered on the French Government from tomorrow, I think we should above all be concerned about the workers who will be asked to work more and more without really having a choice, and also about all those who will be asked to work on Sundays in France.
It is clearly in order to open up this possibility that the French Government has changed its position on the individual opt-out in the Council.

Ladies and gentlemen, let us listen to the workers and try to answer their call. If we do not want the negative votes in the French, Dutch and Irish referenda to be followed by many others, challenging the European Union for giving them the impression that it does not deal with their everyday problems.
Mr President, this was a very fine French Presidency.
There were very few dissenting voices not to agree.
The 27 have overcome their differences to reach a series of decisions that have been exemplary.
Europe is taking its first step on the energy and climate package, and it is a huge one.
It now has the means to assume its environmental responsibility without sacrificing its economy.
It can now take the lead in this area and encourage the other continents to follow the path of sustainable development.
Moreover, Ireland proposes a scenario to get out of its isolation.
Our Irish friends will once again vote on the Lisbon Treaty, with all the cards in their hands, since practically all the other members will have definitely voted.
The current crisis shows that the Union more than ever needs better institutional tools to be effective.
The European Council adopted a massive, coordinated economic recovery plan based on long-term productive investment and targeted support for the most affected industrial sectors.
President Sarkozy raised the issue of reducing certain VAT rates, and I totally agree with him.
Let us hope that the Ministers of Economy and Finance will reach an agreement in March, which seemed impossible to the Heads of State and Government last weekend.
The trials to which Europeans are subjected give the Union the opportunity to reveal itself to their eyes as more united, more reactive, more concerned with protecting them.
We are grateful to the French Presidency for having breathed new life into this.
We want, like Nicolas Sarkozy, to no longer see the Councils of yesterday, which ended at 4 a.m. to negotiate three peanuts.
Over the past six months, Europe has indeed changed its mindset, its scope and its credibility.
We must thank the French Presidency for this. We are well aware that it was not entirely unrelated.
The situation in Gaza is known to everyone.
that we discuss this here in Parliament is natural.

But we are asking the Security Council to take a position, we are asking the bodies to take a position, we are asking the European Union to take a position, and we in Parliament are discussing and do not want to take a position.

I think that, in the light of the urgency of the situation in Gaza, it is extremely important that this Parliament express itself and say exactly what it wants in order to stop the massacres taking place in the Middle East at the moment.
I find it unacceptable that this Parliament does not have the courage and clarity to vote on a resolution after our debate.
That is why our group is asking for the Conference of Presidents ' decision to be revised and for the debate that we must have on this issue to be concluded.
on the situation in Gaza, through a resolution which will demonstrate the clear and firm position of this Parliament, of a majority, to put an end to the massacres in Gaza.

We want a resolution. We must be aware that we have a political responsibility in the face of what is happening, and this political responsibility cannot simply be a discussion. It must be a resolution that clearly states what we want and what we denounce.
Mr President, Commissioner, more than ever, the draft directive that Parliament is about to vote on gives me mixed feelings.
It is true that the rapporteur, Mr Klinz, has been working on this for years and has negotiated it skilfully, particularly during the trialogue.

It is also true that the directive makes a number of significant advances in the direction of better functioning of the internal market and of the collective management of financial assets, and is welcomed as such by the European investment fund industry.

It remains that the debates and discussions in the Council and in Parliament were never really free, since the outcome was known in advance. For certain players, it was imperative to introduce the European passport for management companies at any cost.
On the principle, there is nothing to say: the passport is quite defendable. However, we must ensure that the introduction of the passport for management companies
with the necessary guarantees, particularly in terms of fund supervision, since it leads to a separation of functions across borders.
However, I regret to see that the system put in place is not only complex, but also open to divergent interpretations.
It will soon run into practical difficulties that will harm European industry, particularly in exports to third countries.
I have taken note of my minority position, but I do not feel uncomfortable.
Under normal circumstances, I would have been tempted to say that only practice will allow us to draw definitive conclusions in this matter and therefore to determine
Who is right between those who trust the market players ’ ability to adapt and those who adopt a more cautious approach?
Success is not impossible, but on the other hand, nothing guarantees it either.
However, very recent events, particularly the Madoff scandal and its implications for the financial assets collective management industry, are not reassuring.
My skepticism has given way to concern.
The investment fund industry will not be spared by the financial crisis; we are already seeing that.
Fundamental questioning may be necessary, and from this crisis context,

that in these contexts of deep crises, we make choices that dilute the responsibilities of the actors or that presuppose perfect cooperation between control authorities, can really be seen as incongruous.
I am telling you this because I believe it: this directive is also from a bygone era, from before the crisis.
It is marked by a certain carelessness.
Indeed, the financial system is going through a crisis of confidence that is lasting and fundamental, and entire sections of its architecture must be rethought.

Let us not forget that the primary function of UCITS is to protect investors through risk diversification and the establishment of strict rules. By voting for this text as it stands, without really knowing where we are going, we are acting as if nothing had happened.
This autism is not a good sign, and that is why I will abstain, even though I have signed the compromise amendments negotiated with the Council.
Given the circumstances, the improvements made seem to me to be of little weight in the face of the issues of principle that I have just explained.

Mr President, although the regulation on the reporting of animals has been in force since January 2007, it seems that the Member States have not systematically complied by providing the annual reports requested of them.
A global analysis is therefore still random, as many elements are missing regarding the means put in place to carry out inspections.
As a member of animal protection associations and of the Intergroup on Animal Welfare, I am particularly concerned about this issue.
Associations have been fighting desperately for decades to finally see directives introduced in this area in 2007, where we see that the Member States are showing a certain laxity.
I would even go so far as to say that there is a certain lack of goodwill, because, after all, we know that implementing controls and inspections is not at all insurmountable.
We know the slaughterhouses, we know the farms, we know the transit routes for livestock, so what is the problem?

I believe that in our day and age, when public opinion is becoming increasingly aware, quite rightly, of the condition of animals, whether in terms of breeding, slaughter or transport, it is important that the Member States respect these feelings.
And since I have the floor, I would like to add that, with regard to transport, it would be essential to take into account, independently of transport times, local climatic conditions.
Indeed, a journey of several hours in the Netherlands in the spring is not comparable to the same journey in the height of summer in a country such as Greece.
Should we not impose night-time transport in these last cases?
Thank you for considering this proposal in the future.

Mr President, I am delighted that it is a Briton, our colleague Parish, who has tabled this text, as proof that the Roman soldiers two thousand years ago were right to go to Great Britain and bring amphorae of olive oil and wine.
But then two consequences would have to be drawn.

It is not very reasonable, symbolically, to locate the Food Agency in Helsinki, and it is not reasonable for the European Commission not to reverse its position on the following points: first, to establish aid for almond growers; second, to stop the
attacks on olive trees, particularly in Andalusia and throughout Europe, stop the attacks on tuna fishermen in the Mediterranean and the attacks on European vineyards with uprooting
I suggest that our Commissioner, our President, Mr Barroso, organizes a big banquet in Portugal with sheep's cheese and wine from the south.
Mr President, we have all indeed campaigned for the closure of Guantánamo, and we all welcomed President Obama's decision to close this prison of shame.
Today, the question of accepting the Guantánamo detainees should not be posed to us.
As Europeans, loyal to our values of defending human rights, we must take our responsibilities, because it is our duty to welcome these former detainees.
We have read – and I am addressing the Council here – in the press that some Member States were not very enthusiastic about this reception.
I would therefore like to make a real appeal to the Council and, in particular, to the reluctant Member States, to accept these detainees on their territory.

And I would say to you, ladies and gentlemen, that the worst thing would be for the goodwill generated by the closure of Guantánamo to founder because we had not answered the call when Europe was called upon.

important, because the European animal feed sector is one of the most important sectors of agriculture, both in terms of production, since it represents half of the agricultural production in the European Union, 120 million tonnes, and in terms of turnover, about EUR 50 billion.
The European Union has, in fact, five million breeders and 60 million households owning pets.

In the past, the European Union has had to manage a number of health crises, which means that today we must be more vigilant about transparency in order to meet the expectations of farmers, but also of consumers.

The provisions in this report have the advantage of imposing minimal costs on industry and providing great benefits for consumers, who are increasingly concerned about the quality of the products they buy.
Adoption of this report will limit risks by guaranteeing better product quality, better control, better traceability and greater information for the farmer and, ultimately, for the consumer.
Today, with the increase in international trade, it is more than imperative to strengthen any prevention system so that the food crises we have known in the past do not happen again.

This report manages to reconcile the right to information, while strictly defining the nutritional elements that must necessarily appear on labels, and the right to intellectual property, so important for preserving the competitiveness of our industries.

I believe that manufacturers should indeed be required to immediately notify the use of any new raw materials used in animal foodstuffs, in order to guarantee transparency and facilitate the control carried out by the competent authorities.
The fact that an urgent procedure is included, which allows a new dangerous substance to be introduced into the list of banned substances, seems to me to be absolutely essential.

Likewise, giving the farmer the possibility to question the competent national authority or the European Commission in case of doubt about an unfair allegation helps to better verify the system, protecting the client while preserving the fairness of commercial transactions.

I therefore wish to express my full support for this excellent report, as experience shows that legislation on the labelling of animal feed, which promotes quality, transparency, traceability and control, is the best guarantee for preventing new animal health crises in Europe.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, it is certainly not Europe, and even less so its High Representative for Foreign Affairs, Javier Solana, former Secretary General of NATO, who can play the role of mediator between Israel and the Palestinians.
At most, they are called upon to finance the reconstruction of the Gaza Strip, as they are doing today for Kosovo, Lebanon or Afghanistan.
The Americans and the Israelis bomb, the Europeans finance reconstruction.
What is the division of tasks between the Allies?
But it should be the vandals who pay.
Egypt is at the heart of the peace talks, which are focused on a long-term ceasefire with Hamas.

But the challenge to be met is formidable, in that the new Israeli Government, under pressure from the third man, Mr Lieberman, leader of a far right-wing party which is democratically accepted there, risks having a particularly difficult task in these negotiations.
Indeed, Netanyahu, who is presented as the future Prime Minister, has always opposed a truce with Hamas.
Another difficulty is that Mahmoud Abbas ’ s Palestinian Authority has become, in the West Bank, a kind of international protectorate whose legitimacy has greatly diminished among the population.

The last factor to be taken into account is the expansion of the Israeli settlements, which has never ceased since 1967, and which makes the creation of a Palestinian state in the West Bank particularly delicate.
Today, the ball is in the Israeli camp, but do the hawks on both sides accept this truce from God that both parties are demanding without providing the means for it?
Allow me to add a comment on the reintegration of France into the integrated military structure of NATO, which will be debated with the Vatanen report.
This reintegration will entail serious obligations for France.
Indeed, we are rejoining NATO at a time when the Cold War ended in 1990.
Mr Sarkozy seems to have forgotten the fall of the Berlin Wall and the return of Russia to the ranks of the free nations.
Should we strengthen the logic of blocs at a time when multipolarisation and the growing power of emerging countries, including on the military level, are on the rise?
Furthermore, France ’ s accession to the integrated structure will oblige it to reinforce its contingent in Afghanistan, although it already has 3 300 men on the ground.
With what budget will it be financed, when its defence budget will fall below 2% of GDP and more than 30 regiments will be scrapped?
Paradoxically, we are going to increase our budgetary contribution to integrate NATO and at the same time reduce our military presence in Africa.
European defence, which is so dear to President Sarkozy, will therefore be a pillar of the Atlantic Alliance.
It was enough to read the Treaty of Lisbon and its additional protocols to be convinced.
Whether in foreign policy or in common security, the European path is a dead end that will only lead to alignment with the Americans and their allies.
It is this logic of erasure that we reject in the name of sovereignty and national independence, based in particular on our autonomous nuclear deterrent force.
Madam President, I would like to congratulate our three rapporteurs on the rather remarkable synthesis they have produced of the current state of our common foreign and security policy.

Mr High Representative, as you know better than anyone, Europe is expected today on the world stage to take the place that its economic and demographic power and the richness of its democratic and humanist values assign to it.

It is clear that despite the will regularly expressed by more than two thirds of our European fellow citizens, you said so, certain progress has been made, I agree, over the past ten years, but it is clear that this Europe still does not exist.
If we needed a recent illustration of this, its disappearance in the solution of the new tragedy in the Middle East would be there to prove it.
There was, and there always will be, a need for a European presence in Gaza to play an active role in both helping the population survive and rebuilding the
and in the fight against arms smuggling, which had allowed this territory to be transformed into a base for launching rockets of all calibres.
Despite all the fine words spoken in Sharm el-Sheikh and Jerusalem, nothing has yet been done in this respect.

I am raising here a question that I have already asked during the Lebanese crisis: when can we, Mr High Representative, expect the deployment of a European fleet in the Mediterranean, as we had one in the face of piracy?
We have the means.
Will we ever have the will?
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, Parliament's decision to accept the urgency requested by the Council for the
The treatment of this file has been unanimous, both in the Committee on Fisheries last week and this morning here in plenary.
We are all aware of the need for the European Union to honour the commitments made in Marrakesh last November by the relevant international body, the CICTA.
It is a matter of giving effect to the recommendations adopted by this organisation in order to put an end to the persistent overfishing of bluefin tuna in the
Atlantic east and in the Mediterranean, and to deal with the serious dangers that result for the future of the species and therefore for the fishery itself.
Two of our colleagues from the Committee on Fisheries, Mrs Fraga Estévez and Mr Romeva i Rueda, were present in Marrakesh and will speak about it in the context of this debate.
I would like to stress the importance that our committee attaches to this file, which raises the main issues of the common fisheries policy, namely the management of
the management of the fleet, the respect of international, regional and bilateral agreements, technical measures and, above all, control, I will come back to that later.
This file is therefore typical, and consequently constitutes a test, in our eyes, of the credibility of this common fisheries policy.
For this reason, it was obviously out of the question that the implementation of the new recovery plan should take place without consulting the European Parliament.
I am therefore pleased that the Commission has finally chosen the only legally and politically acceptable way to implement the
recommendation of the EESC, namely that of a proposal for a regulation in proper form, under Article 37 of the Treaty.

The transposition into Community law of the commitments made by the Commission on behalf of the European Union in regional fisheries organisations is not, in fact, a trivial operation, and we insist that it always be carried out under the democratic control of our institution.
On the substance, I welcome the various measures proposed, which, although constraining for our operators, are commensurate with the challenges. And I emphasize that the most
important of these measures are certainly those which concern and will concern control, as it is true that without control no restructuring plan can be effective.
I thank you, Commissioner, for this proposal. I invite you to show as much determination as you have shown in the past.
determination in the implementation of the plan once it has been approved by the Council, as you have shown in concluding a satisfactory agreement at the CICTA level.
Madam President, Commissioner, I regret that the Council and the Commission have not yet begun to define territorial cohesion in concrete terms.
The French Presidency tried, and I would like to welcome this initiative.
Our Parliament continues to insist that this objective should be applicable as from the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon and that its contours should be clarified as soon as possible.

I would like, like Mr van Nistelrooij's excellent report, for territorial cohesion to become the legal basis guaranteeing the harmonious development of all the territories of the Union and enabling us to make the most of the characteristics unique to each European region.
It is essential to better coordinate Community policies in order to maximise the impact of interventions on the ground.
Territorial cohesion is not intended to target exclusively those regions suffering from permanent handicaps.
It must nevertheless focus on the polycentric development of the European Union as a whole, taking into account the characteristics of each territory, while preserving their diversity.
This new concept seems relevant to taking into account the outermost regions in order to ensure them sustainable and balanced growth.

In this respect, I thank the rapporteur for having included in his compromise amendments my requests relating to the specific challenges faced by outermost regions in terms of accessibility and competitiveness, which are key elements of territorial cohesion.

Madam President, reading the Commission ’ s recent communication entitled ‘ The European Regional Development Fund: a tool for Europe ’, it seems to me that it wants to apply to the outermost regions all the
recommendations on improving governance in cohesion policy, thus providing the first, somewhat premature, illustration of the implementation of territorial cohesion.

I hope that the upcoming overseas general assembly in France will proceed in the same way and will take into account the major territorial impact of European policies in favour of the outermost regions, whose added value is indisputable.
and indisputable.
We are amused by the conclusions of the last Presidency.
It resembles the victory communiqués of all the armies in retreat.

They are laughable, even in the repeated assertion that, when the crisis is over, the EU will build a stronger economy for the future, or in the reaffirmation of the Lisbon strategy, which is in full collapse.
Thus, as they lock themselves in their glass towers, the Eurocrats, Europhiles and Europhobes of all kinds end up losing touch with reality.
Everyone knows that the euro area was the first to enter recession after having had the lowest interest rates in the world for eight years.
But it is also the region that is experiencing the greatest difficulties in terms of industrial competitiveness and employment.
Above all, it is she who, by general disarmament, takes away the natural defence from nations and leaves them least armed to react.
In reality, the only merit of this crisis will be to wake up peoples lulled by the ease of financial anticipation and by anti-national propaganda.
Only the Member States will have the legitimate instruments necessary for any major action, and they will have to break out of the EU and euro constraints to do so.

For the French, in any case, it is becoming increasingly clear that there will be no salvation without France, no France without French policy, no French policy without national and popular sovereignty. Thank you, Mr President.

Madam President, I think that the question of the distribution of food aid to the poorest is entirely appropriate, but I think that, at bottom, the Commission proposal does not correspond to the problem.
The question of co-financing puzzles me, and I think it is rather the question of liberalism that has presided over the evaluation of the CAP in recent years that needs to be called into question.
The riots against hunger and the sudden fluctuations in food prices are permanent indicators of the limits of liberalising agricultural markets.
This liberalisation has always been to the detriment of vulnerable populations, vulnerable producers and vulnerable regions.

I therefore plead for politics to reassert its rights and take back the reins, both at European and world level, and for the strategic sector of agriculture and of feeding people, both quantitatively and qualitatively, not to be subject to the strict laws of the market.
Certainly, there was an urgent need, and the Sikorski report responds to this need, which I welcome, but I would like the forthcoming debate on the
future of the CAP, it should be recalled that the priority objective is to combat, in a structural way, the hunger and poverty that we know of in all the Member States and in the world.
Rural areas are able to meet food needs with sufficient and quality production, but we still need to give them the financial and human resources to shoulder this heavy responsibility.
Mr President, in this period of economic recession, it was important that the European Union continue to show its solidarity with developing countries.
Let us remember that in 2007, the European Community and the Member States together contributed 60% of total aid for development, making the European Union the world ’ s leading donor.
The European Development Funds help to combat poverty while promoting economic development and democracy.
I am therefore pleased that EUR 23 billion has been allocated to ACP countries for the 2008-2013 period under the 10th EDF, which is almost double the amount provided for under the 9th EDF.
The Court of Auditors, in its report, concludes that the accounts of the seventh, eighth and ninth EDF are, in general, reliable, regular and legal.
We can only welcome the record levels of contract implementation and payments made under the FEADER programme.
However, I also note that progress must be made in strengthening the monitoring and surveillance system, as there is still a significant level of errors affecting certain transactions.
Likewise, there is too often a high level of risk in budgetary support, and in my opinion, we need to assess these risks better.
On this point, I agree with the idea that budgetary aid should only be granted if the recipient country has the capacity to manage the funds in a transparent, responsible and efficient manner.

Among the priorities on which we must work in the future, I would like to mention the integration of the European Fund for Sustainable Development into the EU general budget, as this would increase the efficiency and transparency of development aid.

To conclude, Mr President, I would like to thank the rapporteur for his excellent report, the representatives of the Court, but also all the Commission staff who did an excellent job on the spot, and I obviously regret the absence of the Council, and in particular of the Czech Presidency.
Madam President, Parliament and its rapporteur were forced to reject a bad compromise, and they were right to do so.
Our delegation has remained faithful to the position adopted by a large majority in plenary, which called for the eventual abolition of all derogations from the legal working week.
But the truth is that Parliament was the only one to want to move things forward.
The representatives of the European people had decided, well beyond party lines, that this anachronism called the 'opt-out' should end.
We were the only ones to propose a real compromise text, which the Council of Ministers ignored superbly, as did the Commission, incidentally.
The Council and the Commission have aligned themselves with those who are fiercely opposed to any progress on workers ’ rights in Europe.
Mr President of the Council, it is clear that you have come to the end of your tether.
The Court of Justice obliges you to consider all standby time as working time, so you have not lost anything on that front.
The opt-out you want still exists in practice: the real estate lobby has therefore won.
More than ever, the MEPs elected in June will have to fight to move towards a harmonisation of social standards from the top down.
Uh, Mr.

Mr President, this is certainly not in the name of the LIBE Committee, but probably in the name of my group. I would like to take this brief minute to express my extremely positive opinion on Mrs Paganotti 's report.
Congratulations, Madam, on your report.

I believe that, in writing this report, you have drawn up an extraordinarily precise specification of the elements that should be properly included, Commissioner, in the Stockholm Programme, which I know the Commission is actively preparing.

Allow me to add to all that has been said about the importance of training magistrates, the importance of evaluation, and to stress two fundamental elements in terms of mutual trust, which are at the basis of what could be mutual recognition in the future.
Firstly, the independence of the judiciary.
At the moment, there are a number of countries in the Union where the independence of the judiciary from the political authorities or from other powers is not established.
It is a scandal, and this scandal must end.
Secondly, procedural guarantees.

Until we are sure that in a certain number of countries people who are suspected or accused of having committed certain types of offence enjoy procedural guarantees similar to those that exist in other countries, it is difficult to widely accept the principle of mutual recognition.
This is a fundamental point and I wanted to introduce it into this debate.
Congratulations, Mrs Pagano.
Madam President, we cannot remain silent on the situation in Madagascar, and we will not be alone in condemning it.
the African Union, the Southern African Development Community, the International Organisation of La Francophonie, the Inter-Parliamentary Union, the European Union, the Commission, the

The United States, a large number of countries, including my own and Norway, to mention countries from the European continent, have condemned the coup d ' état, because we must call a spade a spade, which took place in Madagascar.
We cannot remain silent on this, and we are calling for a return to constitutional legality.
We never ask for a return to square one, with, if necessary, an arbitration by the Malagasy people through a consultation in the form of a presidential election or a referendum.
It is up to Madagascar's assemblies and political leaders to determine the most effective form of consultation.
So what we are asking for in this joint resolution is to add our voice to that of the international community to clearly signal to those who have seized power in a completely illegal manner

democratic and in the form of a coup d'état, however well disguised, to return to the constitutional legality of Madagascar, which is one of the guarantors of the future development of this great island in the Indian Ocean.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, I would like to thank Commissioner Dimas for his presence, for his answers and for the attention he continues to give to all these disasters.
We worked together a great deal when President Barroso asked me to prepare a report, which he has just mentioned, on the creation of a European Civil Protection Force.
I presented this report on 9 May 2006.
When I read it again and see all the disasters that have happened, I think it is still relevant.
Madam Zavella, obviously, there is not only Greece.
All countries are affected. And I would add that there are not only natural disasters, there are also human disasters caused by people.
I am thinking of maritime disasters, the Erika and many others, of industrial accidents such as Chernobyl.
There are other natural disasters than fires.
I am thinking of the tsunami.
We can fear a tsunami in the Mediterranean one day, as there was one at the beginning of the 20th century which totally destroyed the city of Messina.

Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, frankly, the tools we are mobilising are not up to the job of dealing with these disasters, which will increase in number due to the double effect of climate change and transport.
That is why I think we need to be more ambitious.
I would like to thank the Commission, Commissioner Dimas, all the teams and the Directorate-General which run the Civil Protection mechanism.
I think we need to go further.
With the Member States that wish to do so, we can imagine enhanced cooperation.
If we are not 27 to create this civil protection force, let us begin with 12 or 15.
And then you'll see that it will concern everyone.
When I list the disasters, I can say that everyone is affected: the floods that hit Germany in 2002, and even the major pandemics, and also terrorism.
That is why, Commissioner, I would like the Commission to take the initiative to go further, to propose to the Member States that they go further.
Our Parliament, the Committee on the Environment, the Committee on Defence and Security will support you in these voluntary initiatives.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, once again this summer, Mediterranean forests have been ravaged by fire, in Marseille, in southern Corsica, but also in Italy, in Spain and, on a larger scale, in Greece.
More than 400 000 hectares of forest and fields have gone up in smoke, causing considerable material damage, irreparable damage to biodiversity and dramatic loss of human life.
Shocked by this repeated toll, year after year, and aware of the multiplying effect of climate change on fires,
I am calling for a European awareness campaign to improve coordination of efforts and pooling of firefighting resources.
I would like to see the realisation of a large-scale Euro-Mediterranean cooperation project to combat forest fires within the framework of the Union for the Mediterranean.
I welcome the existing initiatives, and in particular the European Forest Fire Information System pilot project.
I nevertheless demand a reform of the European Union Solidarity Fund so that it becomes an effective instrument for crisis response and a reinforcement of the European force of
protection and the Euroaid system imagined by Michel Barnier as early as 2006, to intervene on the territory of the 27 Member States as well as abroad.

I believe that one of the keys to the problem of forest fires lies in the exchange of knowledge and best practice in terms of prevention, and I would like to highlight the pioneering role played by the forest firefighters in my own department.
I can obviously make this example available to Europe, as it can be of use.
Madam President, Commissioner, I will be brief.
I think there are two elements in this debate.
The cyclical issue: how to get out of this crisis.
All the measures, from aid to farmers to intervention measures, are necessary and absolutely necessary.
They must be supported and we must even consider making them greater.
That is what we are proposing, along with a number of colleagues here in Parliament.
Secondly, there is a structural question about the management of the milk market, and here is where we differ, Commissioner.
You are proposing to go in the direction of contracting out.
I will tell you in advance that, in the long run, if we contract between industry and agriculture, with industries competing with each other, we will end up with competition on a European scale.
We need public regulation in order to regulate a market.
There is no other alternative.
That is where the debate should begin after this crisis.
I think that in this debate, we must respect all opinions and all options, and I am afraid that we rushed too much at the time of the health check to settle an issue such as quotas, which

up to now, I say this to all those who have spoken, have allowed us to maintain milk production in Europe, to ensure a highly developed dairy industry and, at the same time, to ensure a price for consumers that has so far been entirely acceptable. Thank you, Commissioner.
Mr President, a few days after the positive referendum in Ireland, I really have the impression that we can say that we are today more European than before.
I am truly, fully, serenely delighted.
Unfortunately, we are still essentially dependent on the decision of the Constitutional Court, but also on the good will of the Czech President.

We need to send a clear signal of accountability to those who hold in their hands the future of this treaty, which is vital for the Union, for our institutions and for the 500 million Europeans whom we represent.
We cannot be held hostage any longer by a few people whose only aim is to thwart the general interest of Europe.

If it is legitimate for some to wish not to go any further in European integration, if it is legitimate to express such reservations, it is also legitimate to allow those who wish to do so to progress.
In general, the ratification processes of European treaties too often resemble sagas or Pyrrhic victories.
Are we condemned to constantly have to force the implementation of a treaty and its institutional reforms?
We will have to learn from this chaotic ratification process and also, of course, from this commitment to renegotiate with a view to holding a second referendum.

referendum in Ireland, as if it were normal to make a people that had expressed itself in a sovereign manner vote again, and as if it were normal for assent to a treaty to be used as a bargaining chip.
The coherence and institutional balance of the European project are at stake.
Ratifying a treaty is a minimal requirement of loyalty and cohesion from the moment you choose for your country to join the European Union.
We wanted to send a strong signal to citizens by ratifying the treaty for the June 2009 European elections.
What do you think citizens think of the Treaty of Lisbon today, and what do they think of our internal consistency and our legitimacy?
Mr President, my speech is on the deterioration of working conditions in Europe.
Today, nearly half of the world's workers earn less than two dollars a day.
Twelve million three hundred thousand people are still slaves and more than two hundred million children are forced to work.
In Europe, the number of working poor is increasing day by day and the International Labour Organisation records 160 000 deaths every year due to lack of prevention.
In this context, I would like to stress our responsibility towards all those who are unemployed and deprived of social rights, and who work in degrading conditions.
of these millions of workers who suffer morally and physically, sometimes driven to suicide or victims of cancer or chronic diseases due to their working conditions.
It is time to move away from the religion of working more, from the race for profit, from short-term competition.
The right to decent work and the strengthening of labour rights must be top priorities for the Union in the coming ten years.
Madam President, Mr President of the Council, Mr President of the Commission, a challenge for humanity, a challenge for future generations.

To rise to the challenge, Copenhagen must allow a global agreement, a single agreement, as our Chair of the Committee on the Environment, Mr. Jo Leinen, said earlier. Yes, an alliance of all, industrialised countries and emerging countries, is necessary.

Today, with our debate and also with the drafting of a resolution by our Parliament, we are calling on the European Union once again to maintain its leading role in this necessary policy to combat climate change.
Let us remember that we must speak with one voice in order to maintain our credibility.
Yes, we must reach an agreement in Copenhagen to limit the increase in the world average temperature to a level that does not exceed two degrees above pre-industrial levels.
Yes, we must sign an agreement in Copenhagen to achieve collective reductions in greenhouse gas emissions of 30% by 2020 compared with 1990.
But that will not be enough.
It is not only necessary for developed countries to reduce their emissions considerably, but also for developing countries to contribute to achieving the objectives.

Industrialised countries must therefore provide sufficient, lasting and predictable financial and technical support to developing countries to encourage them to commit to reducing their greenhouse gas emissions.
Copenhagen must therefore enable developing countries to move towards low-carbon models.
Allow me, too, to look at two paths that seem necessary for our future.
Firstly, the setting up, on a global scale, of funding for the fight against climate change based on the taxation of financial transactions.

Then, it seems to me necessary to implement a fiscal adjustment at Europe's borders that will target imports of products manufactured without the slightest concern for environmental protection, a fiscal adjustment linked to

a contractualisation, a transfer to the southern countries of the carbon tax collected at the borders of Europe, and which would serve to finance investments in the equipment necessary to combat global warming.
Mr President, I would like to follow on from my speech yesterday on preparations for the Copenhagen Summit, and also on yesterday's Ecofin Council meeting, which was not a success.
I called on you to shoulder the responsibility that we have as industrialised countries to ensure that developing countries can follow us in our efforts to combat climate change.
In Copenhagen, we cannot act as if we all have the same capabilities, otherwise it would mean that we all start from the same point and end up with nothing.
So, in order to convince our developing partners to join us, we will obviously have to address the issue of aid, which must be granted to them, and not wait for the outcome of the Copenhagen Summit.
At the next European Council, the 27 will absolutely have to agree on the way in which the European Union conceives aid to developing countries in the important context of climate change.
As I said yesterday, the Ecofin Council of Ministers of Finance failed to find a common position on this issue.
Yet we know that this issue is absolutely central.

The European Commission has already made proposals. I absolutely want us to get ahead of the game to lead our partners towards the global solidarity necessary to overcome this problem of climate change.
Mr President, I welcome the efforts of the Swedish Presidency to reach an ambitious agreement in Copenhagen, despite the very strong winds of pessimism blowing over these future negotiations.

As far as I am concerned, I do not think it is realistic to admit at this stage that the Copenhagen Summit is dead in the water or that it will not lead to a conclusive agreement to be ratified by all parties.
Let us not give in too quickly to pessimism.
I would urge that at this stage, we do not lower our ambitions, either in terms of objectives or deadlines.
We must continue to believe in a comprehensive, ambitious and binding global political agreement paving the way for the conclusion of a treaty as soon as possible.
It is really too early to mention the deadline of the COP 16 in December 2010.
We must now turn our rhetoric into real political will.
We need to clarify our financial commitment and our aid to developing countries, particularly in terms of technology transfer.
At this stage, the European Union must make a total and collective commitment.

I also note on paper the recent but exemplary momentum given to the talks by Brazil, South Korea, Indonesia and Norway in particular, which have quantified their ambitions to reduce their polluting emissions.

While we can understand the flexibility requested by the United States, which is only just beginning to take the climate issue into account, we must nevertheless achieve quantified, binding and ambitious commitments, in the short, medium and long term, from other major polluters such as the United States and China.
Otherwise, we are headed for climate, political and moral disaster.
Madam President, we are all aware of, and have all said, how important the Copenhagen negotiations are, but while we are all hoping for an agreement, we cannot accept an agreement at any price.
If the conditions for an ambitious agreement are not met, I very clearly want the European Union to be able to say no and refuse to sign a half-hearted agreement.
The European populations that we represent here are expecting an agreement that will enable us to effectively combat all the climate imbalances that we see around us every day.
They will not be content with press releases, non-specific statements of intent and non-binding objectives, which can be brushed aside once the media spotlight has dimmed.

At Copenhagen, the states will have to make commitments. The European Union, the only continent to have seen its CO emissions fall since 1990, the only continent to have made precise, binding and ambitious commitments for the future, cannot shoulder the burden alone.

That is why, if other industrialised and emerging countries do not want to shoulder their share of the burden, we will have to draw all the consequences by introducing a tax at our borders to protect our industry from competition from those who decide not to participate in a global agreement.
We Europeans ask more and more of our industries every day.
I have the climate package as proof of this, which was signed under the French Presidency.

I would cite as proof the national initiatives, which, like the carbon tax, which Mr Pargneaux welcomed earlier, and for which I am grateful, are pushing for the introduction of environmental taxation.

A sector such as transport, which is among the major emitters of CO, even if certain modes of transport such as aviation emit fewer greenhouse gases, is contributing more and more to the fight against climate change.
Yet this is a sector that has been hit very hard by the crisis.

If, as I hope, maritime and air transport are included in the negotiations in Copenhagen, let us ensure that what is required of European industries is also required of those in other industrialised countries.
In Copenhagen, the world's public opinion will be watching its ruling class. Do not let us down. Thank you.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, the Copenhagen Summit is a historic opportunity to change the course of the planet.
We must reach decisions that are even more binding than those taken in Kyoto.
We too must call on our leaders to create an international organization such as the WTO to regulate issues of climate change and the environment, to create a fund
adaptation to climate change, in addition to public development aid, to create a maritime and air carbon tax, as we must also do on financial transactions.
We can clearly see the pressing need for global regulation of the environment, just as there is for financial markets and the Internet.
We have the opportunity to open this march of history through the environment.
Let us do so, in line with our highest human aspirations, otherwise we will appear as leaders with no grasp of the reality of things. Thank you.

Madam President, President of the Council, President of the Commission, ladies and gentlemen, with less than two weeks to go before the Copenhagen conference, concerns are growing about the success of the climate change negotiations.
Some people are already saying that it will be a failure.
The world's major powers are still refusing to show the ambition that climate urgency demands.

Madam President, by adopting the motion for a resolution tomorrow on the European Union strategy before the Copenhagen conference, we Members of the European Parliament must show that our determination is total and that the conference must lead to an agreement that is, certainly, precise, but above all binding.
Moreover, the binding nature of this agreement that will be reached must not be able to be called into question.

We cannot indefinitely postpone decision-making until tomorrow, otherwise it will be too late. Hence the need to create a world environmental organisation, which today appears not only necessary but also urgent, since it will be called upon, under the auspices of the United Nations, to monitor the implementation of the commitments made in Copenhagen.
We must be able to expect greater patience from the world, more lucidity and more responsibility from Copenhagen.
Mr President, I would like to express my support for the joint initiative by the British Socialist Government and the French Government to send back Afghans in an illegal situation to their country.
Indeed, contrary to what some people have said, these people had seen their applications for political asylum rejected, and this bilateral initiative must now be extended at European level.

Migration pressure is a challenge that we must collectively rise to meet, and that is the sense of the pact on immigration and asylum voted on a few months ago, adopted a few months ago under the French Presidency.
We must now go further by pooling our resources and organising joint return flights financed by Frontex.

The decision by the 27 to ask the Commission to carry out a study on this subject is a first step, and, Commissioner, I would like the Commission to support this proposal, because combating illegal immigration is what the public, and particularly the regular immigrant population, expect.
Mr President, I would like to refer back to a recent event, the Swiss vote on minarets.
Certainly, this vote took place in a non-member State of the European Union, but it is clear that it concerns an issue that is not foreign to us.
I would like to return to this event, because I also see it as an excuse to talk about secularism, and we never talk enough about secularism in this Chamber.
It is necessary and urgent that the principle of secularism dominate the organisation of our society, and I would like to say a few simple things here.
First of all, the opposite of secularism is not religiosity, or even less spirituality.
The principle contrary to secularism is religious communitarianism, which imposes rules and laws superior to civil laws, communitarianism which confines individuals within groups below society.
Our Union, through its history, is multicultural and multiethnic.
It is, and it will be more and more so, and only secularism can allow individuals
Mr President, the SESAR programme, which aims to modernise air traffic management in Europe, is a major project.

How is the Commission going to prepare the next phase of the César redeployment, and in particular the funding of this programme, which is essential for the environment as well as for our scientific and technical influence?
Will the Commission follow the recommendations of the report submitted in 2007, according to which a high-level qualified personality should ensure the political follow-up of this programme?

Indeed, it will require overcoming numerous legal, psychological and technical obstacles by unifying national airspace and getting the air traffic controllers of the different countries to work together.
If Europe is not capable of unifying its skies, how could it be capable of building a strong political union?
Mr President, Minister, ladies and gentlemen, the importance of the issue of climate change no longer needs to be proved.

These short-, medium- and long-term impacts are undeniable, even if today the results of the IPCC in a certain national newspaper are front-page news and certainly strengthen the positions of sceptics.
Copenhagen was seen by the world as a unique opportunity for world leaders to bear witness, to act and to correct the effects of climate change.

The European Union was at the forefront of this debate, not only because of our bold legislation to limit the impact of our activities on the climate, but also because Europe is an alliance of countries that can really make a difference.
Certainly, the outcome of the Copenhagen conference can only leave a taste of dissatisfaction, but I refuse to talk about failure.
The world has agreed on the issue of limiting the increase in temperature to two degrees, and this even for a country as reluctant as China.
Brazil has just passed a law aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emissions by almost 39% by 2020, in line with its commitments.
Many others have also shown great ambition in terms of greenhouse gas emissions reduction targets.
I am thinking of Japan and Norway.
Furthermore, we should also highlight the very positive point of the consensus reached on the urgent need for a mechanism to reduce emissions from deforestation.
A specific and unanimous text has been obtained on the setting up of the REDD plus mechanism to reduce deforestation and thus allow the natural storage of CO.
Far from a binding agreement, but clearly in line with a political agreement, the outcome of Copenhagen should lead us to question the reasons for such a letdown.
I went to the Copenhagen conference, like many of my colleagues, with the desire to participate, to help, to see a negotiating Europe, a leading Europe in action.
I was very disappointed because Europe clearly did not have the place it deserved in the negotiations, and even more so in the case of the European Parliament.
To assert itself, Europe must show itself stronger on the subject.
The meeting of our ministers on the environment in Seville did not lead to any results, and our leaders were uncertain and hesitant, and we remain divided on our objectives.

I hope that Mrs Hedegaard, strengthened by these proposals and by the commitments she made before the Committee on the Environment, will be able to raise the level of the European Union ' s ambitions.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, in order to avoid caricatures, and we have heard a few this morning, I would like to recall some objective facts concerning Tunisia, a partner country of the European Union.

The social situation has evolved very favourably, with a spectacular rise in average income per inhabitant, and now the vast majority of Tunisians meet the criteria defining membership of the middle class.

Over 90% of Tunisians have access to social security, and over a quarter of Tunisia ’ s budget is devoted to education, so that this country is among the first to have achieved the Millennium Development Goals in primary education.
Finally, as Mr Michel said earlier, women ' s rights are recognised and guaranteed.
At university, there are more female than male students. Forty percent of professors are women. A quarter of the members of parliament, local elected officials and journalists are women.
Many countries could envy such results.
Mr President, the acts of fanatical criminals who killed Christians in Egypt cannot be attributed to an entire people and its government.
It would be unfair to hold Egypt and the Egyptians responsible for this abominable massacre, the perpetrators of which will be judged.
Let us not confuse the acts of a criminal with the policy of a country, let us not make an amalgam between fanatics and an entire population. Finally, by claiming to interpose
between the Coptic Christians of Egypt and their Muslim compatriots, we are giving arguments to extremists who are trying to present the Christians of the Orient as agents of the West.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, what is happening in Greece, in Portugal, and tomorrow in Spain or Ireland, is scandalous.

These same Anglo-Saxon rating agencies, which complacently rated as AAA toxic assets and saw nothing coming, are today being extremely severe towards the states that came to the rescue of a financial sector that did not deserve it.

The crisis caused by the irresponsibility of the markets and the banks and this massive aid are directly responsible for the worsening of the deficits and public debt that these same markets claim to sanction today.
That is the moralisation of capitalism proclaimed by Mr Sarkozy, the European Union and the G20.

All that the bankers have learned from the crisis is that the taxpayer is an inexhaustible source of profit and insurance. Rumours are circulating that the scale of this panic stems from a large American bank that received government aid

American hedge funds and two hedge funds of the same nationality that want to win at the same time on the exorbitant interest rates imposed on Greece and on the market for credit default swaps, these insurance policies on sovereign debt that are themselves the object of independent speculation.
Regulating hedge funds, which you are afraid to do, or the markets for derivatives, as Mr Barnier proposes, will not be enough.
It is useless to create a European economic government.
The 27 are in the same boat anyway, and solidarity is not an option.
It is the free movement of capital worldwide that must be called into question, otherwise there will be no way of stopping the speculation that is destroying our economies.
Mr President, today, less than 15% of the original stock of bluefin tuna remains.
Faced with such figures, the solution seems obvious.
However, we must not forget the jobs affected by the decision before us today.
It should be recalled that CITES is not intended to ban fishing, but only the international trade in this fish, 80% of which is exported to Japan.

By protecting tuna, we are certainly protecting a species that is dangerously overfished, but we are also encouraging the maintenance of a more balanced, more sustainable fishing activity, fishing aimed at our internal market and creating jobs.

I am in favour of the idea of listing bluefin tuna in Annex I of CITES. And although I think this decision will be positive, the Commission's support for a thorough restructuring of the fisheries sector
My question therefore concerns the terms of this support.
France is requesting an 18-month grace period, accompanied by financial measures for the seafarers and shipowners concerned by the ban on trade.
What is the Commission's view on this?
Furthermore, like many of my colleagues, I am concerned about fairness.
How can we guarantee that boats flying the flags of countries such as Tunisia, Libya or others will apply the ban on international trade with the same rigour as we do?
What will the new control and enforcement measures be?

Our economic activities' sustainability policy must be tangible, sometimes even unpopular in certain sectors, but I hope that the Commission and the Council do not lose sight of the necessary adjustments to implement these measures.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, as the rapporteur said, this draft directive is indeed a follow-up to a vote by Parliament, as you also reminded us, Commissioner.
Yet my group remains extremely critical of this proposal because it risks turning against those whom we want to support.
By wanting to lighten the burden on businesses, we risk depriving them of the conditions of transparency and trust that are essential for their management and the dynamism of their activity.
First of all, I think we need to be clear on the thresholds and on what we mean by 'micro-enterprise'.
We are talking about SMEs with more than ten employees and with a turnover of more than EUR 1 million.
Regardless of the Member State, this represents a very large majority of enterprises.
Certainly, they may be local and have little presence on the Community market, but nevertheless, the need to present accounting rules will not be abolished for them, and these
accounting rules, they will have to face them every time they want to deal with a banking institution, with partners or sometimes negotiate with fiscal, economic and social institutions.
At that point, accounting rules will be imposed on them and they will have to pay more than for regular accounting in order to satisfy criteria that they will not necessarily understand.
I do not see the point of imposing an accounting obligation, then, outside any common framework for the European Union.
This is not simply a matter of competition; it is simply a question of economic integration, of Community law and of equal treatment for all enterprises in the European Union.
That said, simplifying accounting obligations for SMEs is clearly a necessity.
Three initiatives, in fact, are going in this direction, whether it is the proposal we are discussing today, whether it is the international framework, the IFRS,
, whether it is the revision of the fourth and seventh accounting directives, for which the European Commission should soon be making proposals to us.
These proposals were promised to us for 2010, and it seems that they will be extended, but I do not think that this extension of the deadline is a sufficient reason to
