rush into things and do a half-hearted job, because although these three proposals share the same objective, they may not be structured in the same way. It seems to me, Commissioner, that it would be better

and clearer for businesses, and to provide them with a single, comprehensive response that will allow them to reconcile the objective of simplifying the obligations that everyone wants with the reality of the lives of small businesses, which need it.
The current problem is that the European Commission is dragging its feet in proposing a revision of these directives, and that is prejudicial to our reflection.

I therefore call on the European Commission to speed up the dialogue on the issue of exemption, which is currently dividing both the institutions and European economic circles, and to carry out an impact study.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, as my colleague, Mrs Morin-Chartier, and other Members have said, on 27 and 28 February, the violent storm Cynthia hit several French regions, causing 53 deaths and enormous material damage.
The storm was of rare intensity and caused devastating flooding along the French coast.
After Madeira, Europe is once again affected.
Brittany, the region of my colleague and friend Alain Cadec, has been badly hit.
Three French departments have thus been declared to be in a state of natural disaster: Brittany, Poitou-Charentes and Pays de la Loire.
In the face of these disasters, the European Union must show its reactivity and express its solidarity.
It is not only with words and a resolution that we can support the victims of these disasters, but also and above all with financial aid.
I therefore call on the Commission, and my colleague Christophe Béchu supports my initiative, to quickly release the Union's Solidarity Fund to help the affected regions cope with the damage.
This disaster demonstrates the relevance of Michel Barnier ’ s proposal to create a European civil intervention force.

Europeans must act together, because disasters are multiplying and affecting European territories, and particularly our most isolated and vulnerable regions, such as the islands in the Indian Ocean and the Caribbean.

Madam President, I too would like to congratulate our rapporteur, Mr Leichtfried, on his text and on his spirit of cooperation on this issue, and I particularly support his proposals on the funding of aviation safety, because we are talking about aviation safety and not aviation security.

Our committee has been fighting for many years to have the principle accepted that stricter safety measures should be the responsibility of the Member States, and I fully support our rapporteur on this point.
Indeed, security is not just about passengers, but about all citizens, and it is the States ’ responsibility to take measures to combat terrorism.
It is therefore not justified that air passengers should be the only ones to pay for security measures in airports and on board aircraft.
This is all the more so because, in modes of transport such as rail and maritime, the Member States finance, in part or in full, the security measures.
I therefore think that we must remain firm in the face of the Council and defend Amendment 32 of the report adopted by our committee on transport and tourism to the very end.
I have another point to raise briefly.

While I support the progress made in the areas of non-discrimination and transparency that the text will bring, I do not believe that the aim of this directive is to set up a unique and identical European system of security fees.
While requiring compliance with common rules, we must allow Member States to keep their current systems for collecting royalties.
In countries such as Spain, Italy or France, the fees are voted on by Parliament and, in that case, the body that rules on the legality of laws should act as the independent supervisory authority.
This is the purpose of amendment 40, which I hope many of you will support when we vote on it.
Mr President, Commissioner, the report I am presenting to Parliament attempts to explain that, in the fight against climate change, agriculture has a role to play.
Nature is reminding us this week that volcanoes in particular can have a major impact on human activities and on the climate.
But it requires, despite everything, that men and Europe in particular take their responsibilities to fight against what we call climate change.
My report tries to show that agriculture can continue to reduce its carbon emissions, to reduce its consumption of fossil fuels, and above all, from another
to participate very actively in carbon fixation to fight globally on less carbon emissions and more carbon fixed and sequestered.
This report tries to show that in agriculture, we should move from a compartmentalised approach, which consists of opening a directive every time we have a problem,
on soils, on water, on pesticides, and so on, and try to address the agricultural issue with the ambition of treating it in a global, systemic way.

In this approach, we must be able to find ways of ensuring that agriculture, as I said earlier, not only reduces its energy consumption, but at the same time actively contributes to carbon sequestration.
It is particularly the role linked to photosynthesis, to biomass and then, above all, what is close to my heart, linked also to soil, to this capacity that European soils have to fix organic carbon.

I think that the path that I open up in this report, and I hope that it will be followed during the vote that will take place in plenary in Brussels, must allow European agriculture to enter a phase of sustainability, to ensure a

ecological transition to this agriculture, which would put it ahead of a number of other continents or other forms of agriculture, so that it can be economically efficient, ecologically efficient, and I think also, from that point on, socially efficient.

So this report deals with a particular issue, combating climate change and agriculture, but it has the ambition, I hope anyway, of opening up other perspectives to feed, in particular, the debate that will take place on the future of agricultural policy after 2013.

I am addressing myself to the Commission, I will address myself, and I often do so, to the Committee on Agriculture, and I will also address myself to the various ministers when I have the opportunity.
I think we need to take a U-turn.
This 2013 package must be ambitious.
It must be based on objectives that enable citizens to understand why we need a common agricultural policy.
We need a common agricultural policy because Europe's agriculture must ensure food security, but also commit to the path of sustainability.
That is what I propose in this report, and I hope that Parliament will support me on this by a large majority.

Mr President, I would first like to stress that the precautionary principle adopted by most European governments, closing their airspace in a targeted and temporary manner, was a wise and prudent decision.

The safety of our fellow citizens must take precedence over any other consideration, and under these conditions, the attitude of certain airlines demanding the total and immediate reopening of airspace on the basis of one or two test flights is, at the very least, indecent.
I think it was Mr Hänsch who spoke earlier about the complementarity of transport modes, particularly rail, and I would like to take this opportunity to say how much I regret that the air paralysis
in certain countries such as France, the situation has been exacerbated by the disruption of rail transport caused by strikes, which are irresponsible and incomprehensible in these circumstances.
To return to our subject, I would like to welcome the Commission ’ s decision to authorise the granting of public aid to airlines affected by the current disruptions.
This is a sensible decision in a context already marked by the crisis, but these aids must be exceptional.
In this respect, I would like the criteria used to award them to include the exemplary way in which the companies have compensated their customers who were victims of flight cancellations.
Indeed, it is not acceptable for certain companies to abuse the force majeure clause in order to escape their obligation to compensate passengers under Regulation (EC) No 261 / 2004.
Passengers are victims of the current situation and, if no alternative solution is offered to them, they should not have to bear the financial cost as well.
Moreover, travel agents are exempt from the obligation to refund unused flights to passengers.
This is not normal either.

Airline and travel agency insurance covers them in exceptional cases such as the one we have been experiencing for a few days, and we must therefore ensure that passengers are properly compensated for cancelled flights.
Mr President, the press has not been short of words to describe the closure of European airspace and its consequences: chaos, paralysis, cacophony, disaster, catastrophe.
I will not dwell on what has already been said, particularly regarding the scale of the colossal, abyssal financial impact of this crisis, in direct or indirect terms.

While I welcome the possibility of considering exceptional public aid to the air transport sector, which has already been severely damaged since 11 September 2001, I am nonetheless perplexed by the European management of events.

Firstly, knowing that 750 000 European passengers have been affected, many of whom are still stranded all over the world, and knowing that the economic losses are exponential as time goes by, how can you explain that it took so long
not one, two, three or four, but five days for European transport ministers to meet by teleconference to coordinate their actions and decide on the creation of differentiated traffic zones.
Secondly, nobody disputes the need to apply the precautionary principle, but today, as we progressively reauthorise traffic in certain zones,
security, with unchanged weather conditions and the volcano still active, we are entitled to ask what additional safety guarantees for passengers we could have provided earlier, more quickly.

Thirdly, an evolving action model has been developed, as the volcano ’ s activity can change from hour to hour depending on the weather, and this will continue to carry out updated assessments of the safety of the corridors.
Must these test flights be carried out by civil aviation and airlines?

Finally, if this situation persists, worsens or recurs, which is likely, the management model put in place will have to provide for greater coordination between Member States and special operational procedures based on real data, better

co-ordination of other means of transport in exceptional circumstances, but also taking into account the necessary co-ordination of repatriation aid for the tens of thousands of passengers who are stuck and who, too, have the right to information and assistance.
Yet, so far, only isolated and national initiatives have been taken.

Madam President, I would like, in my turn, after other speakers, to express my thanks to our colleague, Mr Méndez de Vigo, who has done a remarkable job under sometimes heated, and always passionate, conditions.
The debates within the Committee on Constitutional Affairs lasted a long time. They were commensurate with the stakes and with the intensity of the interest that, of course, the

European Parliament, in the major issue that concerns us first and foremost, in a perfectly legitimate way, the composition of our Assembly and the modalities of designation of the members of our Assembly.

Once again, the rapporteur has managed to bring together opinions and contributions that were sometimes different and to synthesize them, I would say, into a text that is extremely balanced, the opinions and the views.
I wanted to thank him.

I believe that the recommendations of these two reports, both the one concerning the convening of the IGC and the one concerning the transitional measures for the composition of our Assembly, are imbued with realism, pragmatism, and efficiency.
I believe that this is what, overall, on other issues as well, our fellow citizens expect from Europe.
Mr President, Mr Dalli, Commissioner, this draft report, as presented by Mr Peterle, takes up the line
Directorate-General for Communication of the European Commission and is also inspired by the resolution of the European Parliament of 10 April 2008 on the fight against cancer in our European Union.

Allow me to support the objectives of the European partnership envisaged by the European Commission to fight cancer more effectively, whether it be the importance of prevention and early detection, the development of a new model of cancer prevention and, above all, the reduction of inequalities among the Member States.
I share the concerns expressed in the Commission communication and in the draft report.
I would like to welcome the work accomplished by Mr Peterle, the rapporteur, in drafting this report and the compromise proposals put forward to take up the various amendments.

As shadow rapporteur for the Group of the Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats in the European Parliament, I wanted, among other things, to highlight the following issues. First of all, the increase in annual deaths due to cancer caused by exposure to carcinogenic agents at work, but also the importance of better access to

information on medicines for cancer patients, but also the implementation of the REACH regulation and the regular updating of the list of substances of very high concern, which includes carcinogenic substances, support for initiatives aimed at preventing the importation of goods

containing carcinogenic chemicals, and to strengthen controls on the presence of chemicals within the European Union. And finally, the development of guidelines for a common definition of disability, which could include people suffering from chronic diseases or cancer.
These are the questions we would like to raise, while supporting this draft report.
Madam President, the oil well blowout in the Gulf of Mexico is a real ecological and economic disaster.
It would appear that irregularities have been found in the granting of forest permits by the US Department of the Interior.
This would be an unforgivable lightness, the consequences of which are incalculable and which do not concern only the United States.

When we know that in the world, hundreds of offshore drilling authorizations are granted by States, if these authorizations are granted with the same lightness, it would pose a real serious problem for the whole world.
elected representatives from European outermost regions and overseas countries and territories, composed entirely of islands, or almost entirely of islands, nearly 130 in total.
in total, spread across the three continents, I must express my very great concern for our islands in relation to projects for offshore oil exploitation.
That is why it is imperative that the European Union, which must lead by example, re-examine with the greatest rigour the safety and prevention measures on its own offshore oil platforms.
Moreover, accidental or operational oil spills at sea, not necessarily due to Europe, are estimated at several tens of thousands of tonnes each year.

Would it not be appropriate for the European Union to take initiatives so that offshore platforms are henceforth under the authority of an uncontested international scientific authority, which should be set up in the form of an international authority?
After Copenhagen and in preparation for Cancún, the European Union would do well to initiate real global governance for the research, protection and exploitation of deep-sea resources.
As a continuation of the Barcelona process, the Union for the Mediterranean is an opportunity for the development of our relations with the countries of the southern and eastern Mediterranean basin.
As Mr Peillon's excellent report points out, we cannot ignore either the potential of this region or the dangers it poses.
Yet, let us have the clarity to admit it: since the bombardment of Gaza and with the continuation of the colonisation of Palestinian territories, the Union for the Mediterranean is at a deadlock.
The political aspect is blocked.
We are not even certain that the summit scheduled for next June in Barcelona will actually take place.
I would not be surprised if we learned in the hours or days ahead that it has been postponed sine die.
We must not give up, but the European Union must assume its responsibilities more fully in the search for a political solution.
We cannot allow the United States to have a monopoly on the political management of the crisis in this region, which is ours, while we limit ourselves to contributing to its development.
The development and stability of the Mediterranean are vital to our security and our growth, and for that reason the Union for the Mediterranean remains the best instrument at our disposal.
Mr President, Commissioner, this is an extremely important subject concerning consumer protection.

A recent survey indicates that the situation in terms of food safety has deteriorated over the past ten years in the eyes of Europeans, and one in three consumers remains suspicious of the quality of products.
Updating, simplifying and clarifying food labelling in the European Union is therefore an obligation for better consumer protection.
Let me address two points.
With regard to the colour code, we are in favour of it, provided that it is accompanied by a reference to nutritional values expressed as percentages.
Then, I am opposed to mandatory nutritional labelling for wines and spirits.
We would like a specific regulation on wine and alcoholic drinks to be drawn up within the next three years, rather than the five years proposed by the European Commission.
That is what I wanted to say, Mr President, Commissioner.
Mr President, Baroness Ashton, this attack on civilian ships by Israeli commandos obviously requires an impartial and credible investigation.
But what credit could we give to a Commission that would be at the same time judge and party?
This attack took place in international waters and therefore concerns the international community, and in particular Europe, which borders the Mediterranean.
Europe, which should be heard more and become more strongly involved in the search for a political solution based on two states, each with Jerusalem as its capital.

Europe is making considerable financial efforts to contribute to the development of Palestinian territories, but unfortunately, it is too absent from the political process that should lead to the creation of this Palestinian state.
The goodwill, financial and budgetary, that we are showing is not enough.
We must also express a political will and take the necessary steps to make ourselves heard.
The violent attack on the by the Israeli army has caused outrage and condemnation.
emotion in the face of the nine victims of the attack, civilians, and condemnation, because this raid in international waters constitutes a violation of international law.

In doing so, the Israeli Government is penalising its own people, because, in the end, we see that, with the strengthening of the extremists, the ultimate objective of security in the region is moving further and further away.
Yes, more than ever, the Israeli Government must abandon its policy of force.

I would, however, like to point out that the strong media coverage surrounding the Mavi Marmara flotilla, orchestrated from Turkey, raises doubts about the exclusively humanitarian purpose of this operation.
An investigation seems essential to shed light on the attack, but also on the preparations that preceded this expedition.
I would like the Israeli Government to accept that this investigation includes substantial international participation, so that we can finally find our way back to justice in this region.
Madam President, High Representative, I welcome the new dynamic created by the launch of the Partnership for Modernisation and its pragmatism.
If this new partnership did nothing more, it would at least have the merit of underlining just how much Russia is an indispensable geopolitical partner for the European Union.
I also welcome the positive remarks on cooperation with Russia on crisis management.
A few remarks, however.

While the EU's demands are always put forward, the interests or positions of its partner seem systematically to be undervalued or scorned, whether in the field of energy or in the way it analyses the Eastern Partnership and the Atlanticist aspirations of its closest neighbours.

I also notice that the firmness shown by most of the Member States and the Union in their statements, the virulence of the condemnations of human rights violations, seems to be reserved for Russia alone.

The statements are more restrained, the diplomacy more subtle, as if it were China, for example, and its systematic violations of the rule of law, even just regarding the Laogai, this global factory, in view of the minimum social requirements in Europe, when our French or European workers are unemployed.

Allow me to remind you of the saying, 'ad angusta per angusta', 'through the narrow paths to the summits', because this is how I see Russia's efforts today to free itself from the criminal Marxist poison which, for so many decades, bled and imprisoned this country.
The current Russian Federation has managed to raise the head of a whole nation, despite the difficulties and the important challenges that it still has to face.

So, a little modesty, gentlemen, you Europeanists, especially when the super-state of Europe fails to achieve any of its fine objectives, despite increasingly expensive means and the progressive domination of the states of Western Europe and of Central and Eastern Europe.
I cannot help but recall that this House had more indulgence for the communist Soviet Union than for Russia.

These are often the same people who yesterday campaigned for unilateral disarmament by the West and for participation in Mr Gorbachev 's common house, and who today experience the most virtuous indignation and the greatest modesty towards the EU-Russia partnership.

On this subject, I must inform you, if you are not yet aware, of new historical facts that will emerge as a result of the progressive translation of Soviet secret archives that the courageous Mr. Pavel Stroilov has managed to obtain. These archives have already provided, and will continue to provide, many revelations.

unpleasant surprise for the chiefs of the single-minded thinking on the reality of the end of the Cold War, which was really Mr Gorbachev, and for the arrogance of today's Europeanists, who were then keen to make certain Soviet projects possible.
At the risk of repeating myself, Russia is an essential and strategic partner for the European states, and our relations must be strengthened for the benefit of all.
This country is much closer to us in terms of culture, civilisation, history, geography, mutual interests or common risks than certain States that you are about to admit to the European Union.
Mr President, we often say that necessity is the mother of invention.
In the face of the crisis, it is obvious, I would say, a necessity that has become unavoidable, to innovate and to be creative.

I welcome the step taken by the Council in this regard, a step which I hope will eventually be a giant leap towards real economic governance, because let us not be mistaken: mere coordination of economic policies will remain insufficient.
Could we achieve a real budgetary federation, not forced by circumstances, but because we ourselves had decided on it?
Let us return to the Council decisions, which I am pleased with all the same.
The reinforcement of the preventive arm of the Stability and Growth Pact is an excellent thing, but there is no real budgetary discipline without sanctions for those who infringe it.
They have been strengthened, but not yet specified, which is regrettable.

Otherwise, I would like to express my satisfaction, among other things, with the orientations decided, such as the famous competitiveness scoreboard, because that is what this is all about: reconnecting competitiveness and sustainable growth for all.
We also welcome the project of introducing a bank tax, the acceptance of a tax on financial transactions and the acceleration of the reform of financial regulations.
Mr President of the Council, all these decisions and orientations seemed, a few months or even a few weeks ago, unrealistic, illusory, or even truly hypothetical.
You are right, it is true, it is a profound cultural change on the scale of the policy of the European small steps.
That said, it is now imperative that, in October, the Member States very concretely implement these guidelines.

It will now be up to the Council to decide whether this step is a giant, historic step or simply a pious wish, which we will all bitterly regret, and in particular a majority of citizens will have to suffer on a daily basis. Thank you.

Mr President, we have already discussed this at the Conference of Presidents. Many groups are divided on the Gallo report and, clearly, Parliament, all the groups, still need to discuss it, because there are some contradictory points that the groups have not been able to resolve.
That is why we are asking for the debate and vote on the Gallo report to be postponed until September, or possibly September 2, depending on the Conference of Presidents.
So it is a very simple thing.
Since the fruit is not ripe, let us give it the summer to ripen and let us discuss it in September.
Madam President, I admit that I have a hard time following my colleagues who so like to flex their muscles and boast of their monopoly of our legitimacy vis-à-vis the other institutions of the Union.
Unfortunately, their stance is often laughable.
But when the Members of this House, through their work and their convictions, put forward an informed position, when
We defend what looks like a European general interest against tight-fisted Member States, and the situation changes completely.
In this debate on European supervision, Parliament has played that role.
On the subject of the European Systemic Risk Board, of which I am the shadow rapporteur for my group, I have defended, like the rapporteur, Mrs Goulard, an ambitious line.
I have proposed mechanisms to ensure a certain level of efficiency, and I have stressed the primacy of the European Central Bank.
Why?
because the European Central Bank is the only Community institution in this set-up and because it has shown its seriousness.
By Christian charity, I will not name all the institutions and all the great people who suddenly disappeared during the storm.
We can be happy that we had at least one captain on board at the height of the crisis.
I would therefore be reassured to see, tomorrow, macro-prudential supervision backed by a strong Community institution, while micro-prudential supervision will necessarily remain fragmented.
In these troubled times, the European Systemic Risk Board must get to work as soon as possible.
The future will show whether further adjustments are needed, but it is essential to start from a sound basis, because systemic risks affect the entire European and global banking sector.
Mr President, the final agreement reached on the revision of the directives on industrial emissions is more than acceptable, and I welcome the compromise work that has been done.

This final package takes up the key points of the position that I defended with several of my colleagues in the Committee on the Environment, and which aimed to make this text an ambitious but realistic piece of legislation.
Firstly, maintaining a certain amount of flexibility for businesses and the competent authorities in granting exemptions.
Then, I would like to welcome the new version of the ESN, which has been so controversial and which, from now on, limited to certain sectors, seems much more achievable.

It is also important to stress that the agreement reached allows better articulation with the ETS directive and definitively removes from the text of the new directive the rather irrelevant idea of creating a market for SO2 and NOx quotas.
However, there are a few caveats not to be forgotten, particularly the provisions relating to soil.
As the Soil Directive is still pending, this important issue will unfortunately only be dealt with by Member States that have their own legislation.
Tomorrow, Parliament must adopt the elements of the agreement reached at the end of the trialogue.

Enormous work has been done on all sides in order to avoid sending this text back to conciliation, a text which, I must remind you, is of crucial importance, both for industry and for European environmental policy.
Madam President, every day brings its share of information showing the scale of the ecological disaster caused in the Gulf of Mexico by the rupture of the offshore platform.
But let us not forget that other disasters of this kind are taking place elsewhere.
I am thinking in particular of what is happening today and for a long time already in Nigeria.
That is why, in addition to a moratorium on new drilling, I think we need to promote stricter rules on the management of platforms, not only European rules but also international rules.
As for the European Union, I think it is necessary for it to equip itself in a preventive manner to fight this disaster, and I think it is obvious
also that all this argues, as Mrs Damanaki said, for a firm and resolute choice in favour of clean and renewable energy,
The European Union should lead by example in this respect.

Mr President, Commissioner, reopening negotiations with Mercosur while the European Union is preparing to redefine its agricultural policy reveals a dangerous lack of coherence on the part of the Commission.
Europe already imports 500 000 tonnes of beef.
Opening our market to new imports would be to the detriment of farmers in Europe's disadvantaged areas, without any guarantee of compliance with health and social standards.
We cannot accept that.
Europe buys 38 million tonnes of soya and animal feed.
These imports from Brazil and Argentina monopolise more than 19 million hectares.
They are concentrated in the hands of three companies: Cargill, ADM, Bunge.
Between 2001 and 2004, soybeans destroyed more than 1.2 million hectares of tropical and equatorial forest.
It is one of the main causes of the increase in greenhouse gas emissions and the disappearance of biodiversity.
The European Parliament has decided to produce a report on the re-establishment of autonomy in vegetable proteins for animal feed.
Agriculture that respects the environment and the fight against climate change demand this choice.
These efforts will be rendered null and void by this bilateral agreement.
Once again, the European Commission is selling out its agriculture and its farmers in return for uncertain benefits for service companies.
These concessions will not benefit the peasant families of Mercosur, but the interests of agri-industrial groups.
To convince yourself of this, you only have to read the report by the Tyson Group, an American company, a heavyweight on the international meat market.
who says that he has invested in Brazil to, I quote, and this is in his report, "use this country as an export platform for Europe".
Other companies, such as Doux, had already anticipated this move by buying the Brazilian group Frangosul in 1998.
Many poultry farmers, particularly in Brittany, had suffered as a result of this operation.
Europe seems to want to continue its policy of offshoring by intensifying its attacks
against farmers. We must stop
Mr President, many of my fellow Members are concerned about the consequences for European agriculture of reopening negotiations on an association agreement with Mercosur.
My group and I share these concerns.
It is to be feared, and as a Member for an outermost region, I have experience of banana agreements, that an agreement with Mercosur will deal a hard blow to many European agricultural sectors.
That is why an impact assessment is absolutely necessary.
It is also feared that this agreement will turn MERCOSUR agriculture a little too much towards a productivist agriculture to the detriment of the small holdings that exist in these countries.

A balance must therefore be found so that this agreement does not turn the Latin American and European continents into superpowers rooted essentially in the dogma of mercantilism. Thank you, Mr. President.
Mr President, I would just like to say that we may not have managed our agenda very well today.
If we allow free trade agreements to bring products into Europe that do not respect our social or environmental standards, then the reform of the CAP will be useless.
We cannot continue to be hypocritical by imposing environmental, traceability, health and safety constraints on the one hand,
to our farmers, on the grounds that we need to protect consumers, and on the other hand, allow products that can freely enter our continent without respecting these standards.
The question that is being asked behind the negotiations with Mercosur is indeed this one.

The way things were initiated, whatever we are told about the fact that Parliament was very well informed and that things took place in the greatest transparency and the greatest clarity, does not lead us to give a positive assessment of a negotiation which takes place while, in parallel, negotiations are continuing at the WTO.
In the interests of consistency, of protecting consumers and of protecting our farmers, I do not think we should continue down this road.
Madam President, I would like to salute the work done by the rapporteur, Mr Bové, to salute the Commissioner and then Mr Dess, who preceded me, on the objectives of this report.

I think it is very important that we bear in mind that in the debate we are having, we are trying to solve a problem linked to the volatility of prices in agriculture and linked above all to the fact that when prices
increase, they are very quickly passed on to the distribution level, but when prices fall at the production level, there is no pass on to consumers.
So there is a problem of organisation of the sector, there is a problem of distribution of what we call added value within the sector.

I think that the Bové report offers, on several points, perspectives that should be highlighted – and here I am really addressing the Commission – in order to try to get out of this fundamentally negative spiral.
The first, of course, is the organisation of the sector and the balance of power within the sector. And so,
What proposals do we make so that producers, gathered together, organised, have more weight within the sector to negotiate?
That is the question you raised, Commissioner, regarding the sectoral groups, but it is also a question based on the contractual framework that will be put in place.
I am arguing, and this is in the report, that this contractual basis is a European framework.
We cannot let each country solve this problem.
There must be a European framework.
It must be flexible, but it must exist.
This is a proposal, a path that is inseparable for me.
Secondly, of course, there is the question of price transparency.
I am in favour of setting up this observatory, which is absolutely necessary so that we can inform consumers about price trends.
I support the proposals that have been made and I hope, as Mr Dess said, that there will be a majority to support them.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to welcome this debate, which once again highlights the need for the Union to appreciate and value its regional diversity.
In these times of negotiations on the new 2014-2020 framework programme, I think it is important to stress the arrangements that specific regions should benefit from.
The issues of continuity and territorial cohesion must be at the heart of our concerns.
But the development of mountainous, island or sparsely populated areas requires more than a set of derogatory measures.
We need a horizontal approach.
We must put in place the instruments capable of making all the areas of Community action coexist in synergy in order to strengthen their added value.
Moreover, as a representative of outermost regions, I am eagerly awaiting the new strategy of the Union for the outermost regions, which the Commission services promised us for early 2011.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, I would like to echo my colleague on the absence of the Council from this meeting of Parliament.
I find that a bit flippant on the part of the Council, I must say.
As the next summit on 6 October approaches, China and the European Union must absolutely and resolutely try to move forward together on the issue of climate change.
Last year, at the same time, we were already busy in this very Chamber urging Europe to do everything possible to achieve an ambitious and binding agreement in Copenhagen.
We have seen that the result did not meet our expectations and our ambitions.
The concerns a year later are the same, or even more acute.

In order to be able to compel or convince our partners today, we must show that our determination is absolutely intact, for example, by showing that we are giving ourselves the means to honour our commitments to the countries of the South.
To this end, the implementation of innovative financing – and I heard Mr Sarkozy ’ s speech at the UN yesterday – is becoming an essential necessity.
For example, the introduction of a carbon tax at our borders cannot be ruled out.

It would act as an incentive mechanism for countries that are not going far enough in their climate commitments, and much more than a threat in the end, and a way of protecting our jobs and our companies, especially to better address the post-crisis period.

Madam President, in my opinion, the need to improve family leave is not something that needs to be proven. Raising the minimum threshold for maternity leave is a step forward, a benefit, and we should not be totally demagogic by comparing the economic impact with qualitative benefits that are difficult to quantify.

But the problem is essentially twofold: first, the economic context, which is true, but not sufficient to leave millions of families stranded for decades; second, the legal shortcomings of the report, because the text contains several types of family leave, whose legal bases are incompatible.
Let us take parental leave, which is mentioned in the text on the same footing as maternity and paternity leave.
As an adoptive mother, I personally welcome the desire to give equivalent rights to those of biological mothers, and I represent all those who share this desire.
I am in fact the extra little packet that Mrs Morin mentioned, added to the Estrela report.

If the objective is indeed to strengthen the health and rights of women, of all women in the labour market, then adoptive women who have become mothers, just like all others, have the same rights and the same protection at work.
They are full-fledged mothers like any others, whether they adopt a child under or over the age of 12 months. Please, no discrimination, as stated in the text.
With regard to adoption, I regret that the text has not been examined in depth.
It was not even fed by the Ramboll impact study.
All of this is not very well developed, which is clearly a weak point.

Nevertheless, despite these reservations, I will support Mrs Estrela's report, because beyond economic considerations, there are men and women who must not only take on their parental responsibilities better in a society where

more and more reluctant to have children, and also because it is our duty to ensure that no one has to choose between sacrificing their children for their work or sacrificing their work for their children.
Finally, we do not sit in the Council, but in Parliament. If we, directly elected Members, are not ambitious, tell me who will be?
Madam President, Mr President-in-office of the Council, Commissioner, I would like to intervene and not go back over the debate as it has unfolded so far.
We discussed a 2011 budget, with the constraints that we know, that is to say those of a deep crisis that Europe is going through, with national public deficits, which may lead to two hypotheses.
Do national deficits constrain the European deficit, or do national deficits have to give a new direction and a new impetus to Europe?
That is the question.
I would ask you, Commissioner, above all to try to think, when we discuss a budgetary revision, when we start talking about budgetary perspectives for after 2013, about not
remain totally soaked in the context of the crisis, unless I am to say to myself and to all the citizens of Europe that Europe will definitely remain in the crisis for a long time.
Our whole problem is to manage the crisis and reduce our deficits and our debt, but it is also to prepare for exiting the crisis.
And that is where the budgetary issue arises.

We will only be able to get out of the crisis, and Joseph Daul said this straight away, if the European budget, because it is European, is able to give us the means to regain what we lack today: growth, job creation, innovation and research.

That is the issue, and it is at this level that we must discuss the budget, and not do as we too often do, constantly integrating the issue of the crisis, the issue of the deficits, and here the Council is excellent.

I have noted that in Deauville, Angela Merkel and Nicolas Sarkozy even announced, in advance, a reform of the treaties, without even having discussed it with Mr Van Rompuy or anyone else, in order to sanction all the States that do not respect budgetary discipline tomorrow.

That is where we are at the Council today, whereas Parliament is asking for only one thing, and in particular the Commission, which is to try to drive forward the European project, and that happens through the budget.
You have noted a number of things on which we are entirely in agreement.
More flexibility is needed.
Of course, we must find a way to have a much more flexible budget. This is a necessity, and Europe's reactivity is at stake.
It is also, of course, a question of setting priorities and making the European budget more efficient.
We will all agree on that, all of us. It is a necessity.
But where we need to be clear among ourselves is that we need a timetable. We need to set ourselves objectives, Commissioner. It cannot just remain words, commitments,

texts, which are very interesting and which I agree with practically one hundred percent. But now we will have to get down to the nitty-gritty, and in particular on an essential issue around which we are circling, namely own resources.
How do we get resources for Europe tomorrow, not for Europe, but for all European citizens, and how do we all together find the way to growth and an even stronger Europe tomorrow?
It is up to you, Commissioner.
Mr President, I would like to focus my remarks on the preparations for the upcoming G20 summits.

Everything has been said by the previous speakers on European economic governance, on the need to strengthen European economic governance, to strengthen the Stability and Growth Pact and to better involve the European Parliament and, of course, also the national parliaments in the process.
I would like to quickly mention two challenges facing our nations and the G20 members, which I believe should be addressed at the forthcoming G20 meetings.
The challenge of the currency war and that of the volatility of commodity prices.
I think that on these two issues, which are real threats to global growth and which cause very important imbalances on our planet, the Union

European Union manages to organise itself, to adopt common positions and to give itself the means to speak with one voice at the forthcoming G20 summits in Seoul and in the ones to follow, and more generally on the world stage.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to begin by congratulating our colleague, Mr Feio, on the quality of his report and on the ambitious proposals it contains.
This also shows that the European Parliament is taking its rightful place in a debate that is crucial for the future of Europe, and we can only congratulate ourselves on that.
The Greek crisis has revealed the dysfunctions undermining the economic governance of the European Union.
I take note of Mr Feio's proposal to establish a permanent mechanism for financial stability.
We must also tackle the problem at its root.
For this, I think that the solution also lies in strengthening the Stability and Growth Pact and, in particular, sanctions.
This is essential if we want a lasting improvement in the budgetary situation of the Member States, which is not always popular.
It is restrictive, but we have no choice.

Mr President, I think the report shows clearly the issues involved in the marine environment, in terms of fishing, aquaculture, transport, tourism, energy, mineral and biological resources, and research, combined with climate change.
This is a considerable development potential that we need to secure, protect and promote sustainably, because the future of humanity depends on it.
The report rightly notes that the European Union is the world ’ s leading maritime power and that its outermost regions offer it the largest exclusive economic zone in the world.
In the light of such a situation, it is therefore up to the European Union to implement an ambitious, a major, maritime policy.
If the world's leading maritime power does not do it, who will?
However, we must admit that the resolution does not go very far in this direction.
We cannot claim leadership in world politics with EUR 50 million.
Finally, while the outermost regions have a recognised strategic position, since they are present in all the oceans, it would have been
It would have been wise to give them a major role in a major oceans strategy, which, unfortunately, has not been taken up in the report, and I regret that.
Madam President, Commissioner, I would like to come back to the anti-counterfeiting agreement currently being negotiated, particularly with the United States.
This agreement is also raising questions in the United States, particularly among officials at the US Patent Office.
Moreover, 75 American law professors have just sent a letter to President Obama.
He ruled that the CACTA executive agreement would be contrary to the US Constitution.
Mr President, as you know, the European Parliament is about to adopt a resolution on the ACTA agreement, which is still raising major concerns and is far from being unanimously supported.
among our fellow citizens, I think it would be useful to receive a very clear response from our American counterparts on two points: can ACTA be ratified by the United States?
Is ACTA a binding or voluntary agreement in their eyes?
Indeed, an anti-counterfeiting agreement, from which China, India and Brazil are already absent and which would not be implemented by the United States, would lose a great deal of credibility.
Thank you.

Mr President, throughout this difficult birth of the directive on alternative investment fund managers, I have never stopped pleading for the principle of equal treatment between fund managers, whether they are from the European Union or not.
This principle sounds obvious, but it has run into countless difficulties.
The result presented to us today is satisfactory.
I would like to thank the negotiators and especially our rapporteur, Mr Gauzès, who, after this particularly painful delivery, deserves a substantial maternity leave.
I would like to stress that the issue of the treatment of third countries should be looked at from two angles, whether we are inside or outside the European Union.
Thanks in particular to the European Parliament, all funds sold in the European Union will effectively be subject to comparable regulation.
We can then talk more or less about a level playing field.

It remains that during the transitional period, non-European entities will benefit from the national placement regime, while European funds and managers will be subject to the Directive, which is generally stricter.

The situation is different on markets located outside the European Union. There, European funds and their managers will remain subject to the directive, which is not the case for funds from third countries.
It would then be much more difficult to sell European products in the rest of the world, as they would be less flexible and more expensive.
We must be aware of this disadvantage, even if it is partly inevitable.

As regards deposit takers, knowing that we will also make the exercise more binding, particularly because of the new responsibilities that will be incumbent upon them, the increase should lead to substantial changes in the profession.

Mr President, I am not saying this to show my opposition, but to indicate that this new regulation is not trivial, and that its implementation, which is necessary, will also involve difficulties of which we must be aware.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, particularly at the beginning of the twenty-first century, patients need appropriate information in order to be fully involved in their treatment, to know where to seek care, to decide on the best treatment in agreement with health professionals and to follow the treatment prescribed to the letter.
Today, patients are demanding independent, comparative information adapted to their individual needs.
Unfortunately, the proposals for revision of the directive sent by the European Commission did not properly respond to the needs expressed by European citizens.
These proposals would open the door to direct promotion by pharmaceutical companies, which seems nonsensical in terms of responding to patients ’ real needs.
This operation is of no interest to either citizens or Member States.
It would mean, on the contrary, additional bureaucracy, increased expenditure and putting patients at risk.
That is why we initially presented a proposal to reject these texts, asking the Commission to go back to the drawing board.

This approach, also adopted by my colleague Mr Schlyter from the Group of the Greens / European Free Alliance, enabled our rapporteur, Mr Fechner, whom I salute, to carry out an important revision of the Commission proposals.

At the same time, we tabled around 60 amendments aimed at further protecting the health of our citizens and facilitating patients ’ access to independent, comparative information adapted to everyone ’ s needs.

The adoption of these amendments in the Committee on the Environment, Public Health and Food Safety has made it possible to place greater emphasis on patients ’ rights to information, rather than making the provision of information an option for the pharmaceutical industry.

In the light of the compromise amendments obtained, we have decided to withdraw our proposal to reject the texts, as the substance of the texts has evolved in a good direction over the past few months.
The draft report, as amended, has managed to counterbalance the initial proposals from the Commission.

However, we will continue to refuse to allow health professionals to be used to distribute to their patients brochures and information provided by pharmaceutical companies without any control by health authorities.
Likewise, we will oppose authorising industrial information campaigns, particularly on vaccination.
Information cannot come freely from pharmaceutical companies.

Experience shows that we must be very vigilant, and indeed, and I am concluding, what happened with the Mediator medicines shows how vigilant we must be and how we must ensure that this balance, as we have sought with this draft report, is maintained.
Mr President, Commissioner, Minister, unity and realism will be demonstrated, at least I hope so, in tomorrow's vote on our resolution on the sixteenth Conference of the Parties on climate change.

I therefore hope that our Commissioner in charge will be able to carry the pragmatic objectives that we will have to achieve collectively, but also defend the sine qua non conditions set by Europe that are associated with them.
In these negotiations, we must also not lose sight of the legitimate expectations of fairness from our fellow citizens and our industries.
The world must combat climate change, not just Europe.
Recognising the importance of intellectual property that is too little protected will therefore be essential.
Green technologies must benefit the greatest number, but we must not therefore sell our present and future knowledge cheaply, even for this indispensable fight against climate change.

Likewise, requesting common methods for calculating emissions, as well as verification and publication of data, so that all emitting industries are subject to the same scale, seems to be a perfectly legitimate request, and not a negotiating tactic.
Finally, and this is more controversial, and my colleague raised it earlier, the shortcomings of the current system of clean development mechanisms will also have to be discussed.
Ladies and gentlemen, we are all well aware, unfortunately, that the real negotiating power does not lie with the European Parliament.

However, the willingness of a number of MEPs, including myself, to work and to participate in a positive outcome to this conference must be encouraged and not neglected, as we have felt over the past few days.
If Europe wants to be strong in international dialogue, then the same must apply to all its legislators.
Madam President, the European Union often presents Economic Partnership Agreements as an opportunity for African countries.
She is therefore desperate to see these agreements signed as soon as possible.
But the question we must ask ourselves is why these countries are not rushing to sign agreements that would be so beneficial to them.
Clearly, there are diverging opinions on the quality of these agreements, and as long as these diverging opinions persist, the ACP countries will be reluctant to sign them.
Would it not be appropriate to go further in dialogue and understanding by listening to the concerns and expectations of the ACP countries?
They want to talk about development strategies, while Europe often responds by opening markets.
It is this listening that the ACP-EU Joint Parliamentary Assembly must impose.
I think that the other European institutions should proceed in the same way if we want to build a real strategic partnership between Africa and the European Union.
Thank you. Christians are undoubtedly one of the most persecuted religious groups in the world.

In the Middle East, they are perceived by certain extremist groups as, and I quote, 'legitimate targets', and the objective is their departure, pure and simple, from this region, a region which gave birth to Christianity and in which their historic presence is necessary for a certain balance.
The events at the cathedral in Baghdad are merely the culmination of a situation that we have passively observed for far too long.
The European Union, as a global player and trade partner of many countries in this region, must strongly express its indignation at the situation.
Discrimination of any kind cannot be tolerated.
Democracy will only triumph in this region when the most fundamental human rights, such as religious freedom, are respected.
Colleagues, we are all concerned by the serious crisis currently prevailing in Côte d'Ivoire.

It is clear that the decision by the constitutional council of that country to challenge, in violation of the electoral code, the results of the elections officially proclaimed by the independent electoral commission is unanimously condemned by the international community.

The European Union, the United Nations, the African Union, the Joint Parliamentary Assembly EU-ACP, the Commonwealth and soon, certainly, the European Parliament, are calling for respect for the official proclamation of the results.
election results and the installation of the new President of the Republic as recognised by the Independent Electoral Commission, which, I remind you, is a body validated by the UN.
All the political groups in the European Parliament have expressed, in a joint declaration, their agreement on these essential points.

They also called for the halt of any escalation of tensions in the country that could lead to civil war, which would be a human, social and economic disaster for Côte d'Ivoire, which must obviously be avoided at all costs.
Madam President, I would like to thank our four rapporteurs, who have done a remarkable job and have added significant value to the texts initially proposed by the Commission.
The citizens ’ initiative that we in the EPP are about to vote for with enthusiasm is a major step forward from the Treaty of Lisbon.
Let us not be mistaken: this instrument can truly change the European political landscape, provided we use it responsibly and boldly. I repeat: responsibly and boldly.
It can contribute to bringing European citizens directly into the European political debate.
It can help to bring about a European public opinion, that is to say men and women, citizens of Europe, who debate together, who take a stand together and who unite on truly European issues.
But let us not disappoint our fellow citizens.
Let us urge our Member States to take the implementing measures quickly where necessary. Let us not disappoint our fellow citizens: the reinforcement of European democracy depends on it.
Mr President, I would like, like several of my colleagues, to address the issue of the persecution of Christians throughout the world, particularly Christians in the Middle East.
There is no need to recall the terrible acts recently perpetrated.
I would like to recall very clearly that defending Christians in the Middle East, as everywhere in the world, means advocating religious freedom, diversity of beliefs,

beliefs, it is to advocate freedom, democracy and human rights, which are values at the heart of the European project and which are, of course, and we are all convinced of this here, universal values.
It is really time now, beyond the indignation, the emotion, the solemn condemnations, of course, it is really time now to turn words into deeds.
I therefore urge the authorities of the European Union, the High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy, but also all our leaders, our foreign ministers,
to take specific measures, to implement a precise, concrete action plan to make respect for religious diversity and the fight against all forms of religious persecution a priority.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, Baroness Ashton, we all agree that the situation in the Sahel region has considerably deteriorated.
Allowing a no-go area to be established on Europe ’ s doorstep is a danger that we must fight firmly and without delay.
Extremist groups are multiplying and threatening the lives of civilians.
I, for my part, deplore the loss of human life and the hostage-taking, and I am concerned about the European nationals on the ground.
Until now, Morocco has been a bulwark against all kinds of trafficking from the Sahel.
Nowadays, drug, arms and human traffickers are circumventing Morocco by going through Mauritania to the Canary Islands and on to the European Union.
It is the responsibility of the European Union, it seems to me, to act in order to help these States put an end to these threats.
Europe's security is played out on our territory, but also in our immediate neighbourhood.
It is time for us to act, and I call on the Commission and the Council to implement an action plan to help bring security back to the Sahel.
but I would like to echo the expressions of sympathy and support already voiced for the peoples of Tunisia and Egypt.
He is a symbol of hope for all the defenders of freedom.
I also have a thought for the victims.

I would now like to say that the European Union, which, as we have said, has made itself noticed by its lack of reaction, must be on the side of the Tunisian and Egyptian peoples to help them to open up their countries to reform and to establish democracy.

The European Union has decided to support the Tunisian leaders in initiating a peaceful transition, preparing the organisation of the forthcoming elections, winning freedom and enabling the Tunisian people to find prosperity, development and social peace, an economy that will allow young people to find jobs.

To conclude, I would say that the Union for the Mediterranean has a major challenge to meet, because the Jasmine Revolution has triggered a wave throughout all the states of the Maghreb, the Near East and the Middle East.
It must implement a strong strategy towards these countries.

But be careful: in the face of this legitimate democratic movement, Europe must find a balance between non-interference in internal affairs, support for the legitimate aspirations of peoples and stability.
Commissioner, my colleagues have reminded me on several occasions

At a time when Europe is questioning a common strategy for its supply of raw materials, and rare earths in particular, we cannot overlook the huge potential represented by the intelligent recycling of our waste.

Strengthening European standards to improve the functioning of collection systems, remedying the problems of leaks or illegal exports of WEEE, or even increasing our collection targets, must all be at the heart of this legislation.
Let us not forget the main actor in the success or failure of this regulation: the consumer.
Citizen responsibility is crucial if we want to put in place effective legislation.
And to do this, Mr Karim, the eco-contribution, among other systems, has proved to be one of the most relevant tools.
Producers of electrical and electronic equipment must obviously become more involved in collection and recycling systems, but we cannot absolve citizens of responsibility for the waste they produce.
Our citizens must be actors and not spectators of European environmental policy.
Madam President, President, Commissioner, I would like to share the remark made by Mrs McGuinness on the fact that there is an essential link between the food issue and the agricultural issue.
The issue that concerns us is the rise in food prices and the consequences that this may have on a certain number of populations in the world, but also in Europe.
I would first like to try to share an observation.

The trend towards rising agricultural prices and agricultural commodities is a trend, in my opinion, that is long-term and will last for three essential reasons: demographic evolution in the world and the increase

of the world population. Second element, the fact that we have entered, with climate change, into climatic hazards that are problematic, whether it is drought or flooding. And the third point, because the world is developing too.
With new emerging countries, consumption levels are rising.
The pressure on agricultural markets and agricultural commodities is therefore a serious trend.
It is made worse by price volatility, and price volatility is a consequence of the financialisation of the world economy.
This is an issue that we must also tackle if we want to resolve both the agricultural and food issues.

To do this, what the Commissioner said is important. There are two elements. Yes, we need transparency on stocks, but we also need a public stock policy, both in Europe and global stock management, particularly at the United Nations level.

Secondly, we need to regulate financial markets and financial speculation, particularly by refusing to allow speculative volumes to have no connection whatsoever with the physical quantities traded on markets.

We cannot accept that, on the issue of food, speculators come and speculate without taking into account the consequences that this may have, since this is about the food of the world and of humanity. And I will finish by saying
This leads me to two major consequences. We need a strong European policy, and we reaffirm strategically Europe's commitment to being a global player in food and agriculture.

Secondly, we need to change our development policy so that, on a global scale - and Europe is a player - we invest again in agriculture, because we will need all forms of agriculture to feed the world.
Madam President, Commissioner, as in 2008, the price of agricultural raw materials is soaring on the world markets.
We are only at 3% of the 2008 level.
The fall in production in certain traditional exporting areas due to climate change, the diversion of cereals for the production of agrofuels and speculation
financial crisis, which has accentuated the effects of the rise, are responsible for this situation, as the reports from the IMF and the French authorities show, and as the Commission also says.
The consequences of this new food crisis are hitting hard the 1.2 billion people in the world who live on less than one and a half dollars a day.
Since October, a further 44 million people have joined the ranks of those living below the poverty line.
Two days before the G20 meeting, what is Europe waiting for to take similar measures to those voted in the United States?
Since 13 January 2011, the US administration has required that positions taken by financial funds on agricultural commodities markets be limited and transparent.
This is a first step towards curbing the appetites of speculators.
The impact is also dramatic for European farmers.
They can no longer feed their animals.
Hundreds of farms are bankrupt.
Five years ago, wheat cost EUR 100 a tonne; today it is trading at almost EUR 300.
The situation has therefore completely changed.
The Commission demands that farmers adapt to market signals; it would do well to follow its own advice today.
In the face of this crisis, the European Union cannot wait until 2014 to find solutions.
We must face up to the crisis, and I propose three concrete actions.
Firstly, the creation of stocks of cereals in countries, to make them immediately available to farmers at subsidised prices.
Secondly, the indexation of the prices of meat paid to farmers on the evolution of the prices of livestock feed.
Thirdly, for 2011, a mechanism to transfer a share of the aid intended for cereals to animal production.
Mr President, are we ready in Europe to face a pandemic?
The answer is yes, but there is a but.
The H1N1 flu episode of 2009-2010 showed us that each Member State acted on its own, without much coherence or even solidarity.

As shadow rapporteur on this issue, I think that we had to make these observations, but above all to go further, to be constructive and concrete for our citizens, who, we must say, and this is what I find most regrettable, have undoubtedly lost a little confidence in public health messages.

Mrs Rivasi ’ s report, which will be voted on tomorrow, takes account of these fundamental priorities, which include systematic strengthening of cooperation between Member States, better coordination with the

European health institutions, clarification of the roles of European structures for the management of health risks, and an evaluation of the vaccination and communication strategies implemented in the Member States.
This is therefore a good report, and I congratulate the rapporteur. However, it must obviously not remain a dead letter.
It is the responsibility of our Member States, which retain the bulk of the powers in this area, and it is therefore a real responsibility towards our citizens.
Mr President, I would like to express my deep emotion following the cowardly assassination on 2 March of the Pakistani Minister for Religious Minorities, Mr Bhatti.
This terrorist assassination follows many other assassinations of courageous, tolerant Pakistanis who love human rights, human rights defenders and women's rights defenders, I must say.
Once again, we urgently call on the Pakistani Government to shed light on this cowardly assassination and to ensure effective protection for people threatened by religious fanatical extremists.
I am thinking, of course, first and foremost, but not only, of Mrs Schreyer.
I would also like to once again strongly urge all the competent institutions of the European Union to include in the negotiations for the
prepare future cooperation agreements, particularly the cooperation agreement between the EU and Pakistan, with a clause on respect for religious freedom and respect for human rights.
Mr President, for the record, with the crisis and the bursting of the property and credit bubble, the Irish State had to step in to support the banks.
The Irish public deficit, the cumulative budgetary balance of central, local and particularly social security administrations, was estimated at 32% of GDP in 2010.

If the Irish Government, which can hardly refinance itself on the markets, wishes to access loans from the mechanism and the European Financial Stability Facility, it will have to meet obligations to make social and fiscal changes, even though these areas fall under subsidiarity.
In our opinion, this is not contradictory.
As a doctor, I would say that in the case of fever, it is pointless to blindly prescribe antipyretics without trying to treat the cause.
In the event that these funds are allocated, it is up to that State to consolidate its finances in order to close its excessive deficit and correct its previous errors.
This austerity plan should increase GDP by 10% over the next four years.

Just as a private individual would not allow a bank to interfere in his private life and management, and would condition his personal loan on a guarantee of solvency, so the European Union can lend to Ireland on the basis of consolidated solvency.
Without intruding into his private affairs, the bank will encourage the individual to negotiate, for example, a pay rise or to borrow elsewhere.

This is how, by respecting subsidiarity, the European Union, on the basis of a memorandum established with the IMF, considers that this solvency passes through the reduction of the minimum wage and an adjustment of pensions, without exempting itself from fiscal measures.
It is up to Ireland to decide whether or not to implement these measures.

This is not a free-for-all for European Union interference in areas of subsidiarity, which would, for example, dangerously impose a European minimum wage, but a protection of the stability of the eurozone through a process of mutual guarantees.

We should of course help Ireland, like other countries in difficulty, which also protects us, but not at any price, particularly the price of seeing a state unable to repay its debts, thus weakening its citizens and those of the whole of the European Union.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, the eruption of the Icelandic volcano last April, as many of us said earlier, provided a painful illustration of the fragmentation and ineffectiveness of European airspace.
Each of us in this Chamber has been directly affected.
Five million of our fellow citizens found themselves stranded, with no real alternative transport solutions.
One year after this exceptional event, new European measures are still awaited.

A first crisis exercise, as you said, Commissioner, on volcanic ash is scheduled for 13 and 14 April under the coordination of Eurocontrol, the OACI and EADT, but it is essential that EASA take real leadership in coordinating safety measures at European level.
It is certain that the information provided by the London and Toulouse EVACs must be refined by using more modern technological means.

As we saw last April, these data can, however, be contradictory and can lead each Member State to take decisions on the closure of their airspace that are less motivated by information than by emotion.
In this context, the role of the EASA is essential in proposing a harmonised interpretation of these data, as well as in ensuring the centralisation and proper dissemination of information.
Beyond the information on volcanic ash, the EASA must set up a data collection mechanism, closely involving the Member States, the airlines and their pilots.
This centralisation of information and coordination of all the parties involved will prevent the chaos and inconsistencies that we witnessed last year.

I therefore expect the Commission, together with the European Aviation Safety Agency (EASA), to present concrete proposals quickly, which will be fully integrated into the implementation of the single European sky.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, this text is important.
It is not a revolution. We cannot say that everything was bad before and that everything is perfect now.
But still, it presents a number of major advances at this preliminary stage of our work.
The main one, in my opinion, is better regulation of distance selling, and in particular on the Internet, and that is Chapter Three, because we need to strengthen consumer rights in this area.
e-commerce is very useful. It broadens consumer choice, enables them to obtain the lowest prices, and it also enables small and medium-sized enterprises and artisans to find new outlets.
To make progress in this area, we had to do it in a truly European way, that is to say, on a few very specific points, to set up a complete harmonisation of everyone's rights and duties.
Very concretely, a client in Italy who buys on a Belgian or German website must know that from now on, he will enjoy the same rights and the same protection
That is what this directive is going to make possible.
This means that the withdrawal period will be 14 days everywhere in the Union.
This means that he will immediately know the total amount he will have to pay, without fear of a nasty surprise afterwards.
This means that by double-clicking, he will correctly verify his order.
Obviously, all this is going to upset certain habits.
In many states, there are nuances in national law that everyone is convinced are fundamental, while sometimes you can't even find a trace of them in neighbouring countries.
But living together in Europe means learning to change one's habits a little in the service of the community interest and the general interest.
Let us be clear: this text will need to be improved further, and we will have to do so in the coming weeks, with the Council and with the Commission.
I welcome the recent progress made in this respect.
But we have already done substantial work, thanks to our rapporteur, Andreas Schwab, whom I warmly thank.
But the real fruit of this work is that the amendments will be voted on tomorrow by all the political groups.
Consumers ' confidence in the EU internal market is very fragile.
We needed this political consensus to preserve it and to hope to strengthen it.
At point seven, after the first paragraph, a first dash: to guarantee respect for human rights and fundamental freedoms, in particular the
freedom of speech and assembly, and the right to peacefully demonstrate, and to listen to and take into account the legitimate demands of the demonstrators and ensure their safety.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, the open skies agreement between Europe and the United States is a step forward, but it is only a step, a step which places Europe in a dilemma.
The Committee on Transport and the Commission have themselves stressed this point.
Either we accept an agreement that disadvantages European businesses or we suspend the agreement and risk losing the benefits we have enjoyed since 2008.
Allow me, then, to draw a parallel with the private and professional pilot licences, a text which will be discussed before the summer. Indeed, pilots, of which I am one, are faced with a dilemma.
The European Aviation Safety Agency, wishing to harmonise licences throughout Europe, will do so to the detriment of the 80 000 to 90 000 general aviation pilots,
directly affected, some of them holding only one American licence, and to the detriment, and this is important, of the businesses and jobs linked to this activity.
I would like to draw your attention to this project, which has no security basis.
There are 31 times fewer accidents among holders of American licences.
The objectives of the basic regulation text 216 / 2008 allow for the recognition and equivalence of licences, and there is no point in waiting for hypothetical agreements postponed until 2014.

I therefore propose an open sky agreement to you and even invite you to accompany me when I come to Parliament sometimes, flying myself, to demonstrate to you that an American pilot's licence is perfectly safe.
Madam President, at this stage in our debate, I am inevitably going to repeat or reinforce some of the things that have already been said.

But I also wanted to say that, like several of my colleagues, I believe that the problems that we are currently facing, that the whole of the European Union is currently facing, cannot be solved by any Member State alone.
No Member State should be left alone to face the challenges it is facing, challenges which, once again, in my opinion, are challenges facing the whole of the European Union.
I am therefore calling for the European Union to have the ability to organise itself and to respond in a common and solidarity-based manner.
I believe that the time has now really come to lay the foundations for a genuine European immigration and asylum policy.
More than a year ago, we adopted the European Pact on Immigration and Asylum.
The time has now come, I think, to implement it in a very concrete way.
We have heard you well, Commissioner, and we have heard the recommendations you have made.
I wanted to come back to this and strongly support some of these proposals.
Yes, Frontex must be given more resources at all costs.
Frontex needs to be given more human, material and financial resources so that it can provide more support for the Hermes and Poseidon operations.
Yes, additional financial resources are needed for Member States that are facing, I would say, first and foremost, the increased migratory flows that we are experiencing today.
This will be the financial expression of our solidarity.
Yes, we need to establish closer cooperation with the countries on the southern shore of the Mediterranean, to support them, of course, to help them, to find instruments.
The Commissioner mentioned, I believe she went to Tunisia to talk about it, mobility partnerships.
Yes, we need to strengthen these instruments in a spirit of responsibility, in a spirit of solidarity, but also in a precise and concrete spirit.

Mr President, Commissioner, you are doubtless aware that the most heated debates we have had here on your communication concern the place of public services, services of general interest, in the single market and their legislative translation.
We have reached a compromise formulation which invites you to go beyond your proposal of 25 and we ask you to go beyond a simple

communication that would not meet the expectations and needs of the stakeholders, of the users, of all the stakeholders of public services, and that would not meet the objectives that you are setting yourselves.
The European Union must send a clear and unambiguous message on public services.
To do this, we must create a positive legal framework, whether a regulation or a directive.
The Treaty of Lisbon gives us the legal basis.
It is up to you to translate them into legislative proposals now.
I think Parliament is ready to follow you down this road.
