Mr President, first of all, congratulations to all the rapporteurs for their enormous work on this discharge, and I also welcome the presence of the Council for this debate.
The seat of the European Parliament in Strasbourg is an issue of European identity, and it is not just an economic issue.

I am talking about this today in this Chamber, on the occasion of the debate on the 2009 discharge, because the symbol of Franco-German reconciliation is being treated by some members of the Committee on Budgetary Control as a gimmick, a simple way of making savings.
I am also interested in the question of Parliament ’ s expenditure.
In this respect, I propose that we hold our group meetings in Strasbourg rather than in Brussels, which would allow us to make better use of the Parliament ’ s seat in Strasbourg.
Since we want to make savings, I suggest that we do other things, such as removing the Friday allowance for our colleagues, and also removing allowances for journalists.
Europe is attractive enough that we do not have to pay journalists per diem.

Let us not hide behind the budget argument to appropriate an important issue that goes beyond the scope of budgetary control, even though I welcome the important work done by our rapporteur, Mr Wille, on this discharge.
Finally, since we are talking about the economy, I would also like to see the system of compensation for groups of sponsored visitors cleaned up and made more moral.

I do not think it is very moral, and it gives a very bad image of our institution, that the group leaders should be reimbursed in cash on the day of the visit, without any work, without any control of what the group has spent.
Sometimes, the amount allocated, I would point out, exceeds the amount of actual expenditure.

It would be logical for groups to send a quote in advance to the Visits Service, to receive an advance from Parliament on expenses, on a bank account, and to pay the balance on presentation of invoices actually paid, with a ceiling, obviously.
Madam President, I would like to address the Commission and my colleagues. I welcome the presence of the Council.
The question that arises today stems from the management of the Schengen area in 2011.

This is no longer the area of the five Member States of the 1980s; on the contrary, it is an area that has continued to expand in recent years and now includes 25 States, three of which are not members of the European Union.
Like all European policies, Schengen cooperation must therefore adapt to the reality it covers today, which is no longer the same as it was ten years ago.

It is in this sense that we must interpret the proposals from the Italian and French Governments and the pragmatic and necessary responses provided today by the Commission in its communication on immigration.
Mutual trust between the Schengen States is certainly the cornerstone of Schengen cooperation.
But this confidence is based on mutual obligations.
If one of the parties does not respect its obligations and creates air bridges, the whole system becomes flawed.
Other Member States must therefore be able to respond to this failure by re-establishing temporary and strictly limited controls at their borders.
In doing so, these failing States will clearly be invited to better control their external borders.

Moreover, as presented by the Commission, these measures would fully respect the spirit of Schengen, in so far as these controls would be reintroduced as a last resort and the decision would be taken at European level and not at intergovernmental level.
Such measures will be intrinsically linked to all the long-term measures envisaged in the context of a common immigration policy.
Frontex must be strengthened and more solidarity must be provided to support the Member States on the external borders.
The situation in the Mediterranean over recent years has merely highlighted a flaw in the Schengen area.
Madam President, in a few weeks, migrants from Mediterranean border countries have represented a significant percentage of annual immigration into France.
This heritage is not static, but must on the contrary evolve.
I hope that these proposals will be adopted quickly, so as to ensure as soon as possible better management of our Schengen area and thus ensure the security of our common external borders.
Mr President, the European Union has finally imposed sanctions on Syria.
But unfortunately, it seems to be doing so with a trembling hand, carefully avoiding challenging the head of the Syrian regime.
This lack of determination and clarity is obviously interpreted by the demonstrators as a form of abandonment and by the authorities as indulgence, which allows them to intensify the repression.
repression, a repression so cruel that one cannot imagine for a moment that, at the same time, Syria could be elected to sit on the Human Rights Council of the United Nations.
United Nations, while today the Syrian authorities are denying access to the city of Deraa, where terrible violence is being exercised against defenceless civilians, to the United Nations observers.

Syria at the Human Rights Council would be an insult to the victims and their families, an encouragement to this regime to use brute force against its people, in contempt of the very values that the Human Rights Council is supposed to safeguard.
So, Madam High Representative, what diplomatic options do you envisage to avoid such a disaster?
Madam President, Madam Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to congratulate my colleague, Carlos Coelho, on this report. He has done a masterly job, and God knows it was very complicated.
Bulgaria and Romania have made considerable efforts to strengthen control of their borders, and they have succeeded.
Whether it is police and judicial cooperation, data protection, the visa policy or the control of air, sea or land borders, the latest assessments are very clear.
All the technical criteria are met.
We can only accept this as a fait accompli.
Bulgaria and Romania are now able to implement their Schengen acquis, both technically and in terms of staff.
Moreover, although no obstacles currently prevent the enlargement of the Schengen area, we note that a number of recommendations have been made to these two countries.

I hope that these recommendations will be closely monitored, particularly with regard to the borders between Bulgaria, Greece and Turkey, because, as you know, mutual trust between Schengen countries depends on these countries fully respecting their reciprocal obligations.
If one of the parties does not respect its obligations, it creates air gaps and the whole system becomes defective.
I very much hope that an agreement will be reached in the Council so as to welcome these two countries into the privileged circle that is the Schengen area.
We all appreciate in this Chamber the impact of this type of decision on the citizens concerned.
Once again, congratulations to Mr Coelho on his report. Thank you, Mr President.
Mr President, in its Europe 2020 strategy, the European Union has set itself the objective of making our economy greener and our transport more modern.
All the European institutions have strongly supported this objective, including the vast majority of Member States and our Parliament.
It is therefore regrettable that, since 2008, several Member States have been trying to empty the new Eurovignette proposal of all its content and have blocked this file.

Today, however, thanks to the tenacity of our rapporteur – and I would like to pay tribute here to Mr El Khadraoui – and to the tenacity of the shadow rapporteurs in their negotiations with the Council, we finally have a compromise before us.

This text is certainly much less ambitious than the initial proposal, but it will allow, for the first time, the principle of 'polluter pays' to be put into practice and will internalise the external costs of transport, such as noise and CO emissions.

In order for this Eurovignette not to be a mere tax, but to actually contribute to improving transport in Europe, Member States are also encouraged to reinvest the money collected in modernising transport modes.
Parliament has also succeeded in obtaining tangible advances in terms of interoperability of toll systems, as well as stronger incentives for the use of less polluting heavy goods vehicles.

In order to remain consistent with the political objectives that our institution has set itself, I sincerely hope that we will be able to adopt the compromise proposed to us at second reading and thus pave the way for a more sustainable transport policy.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, yes, I agree that there should be equality with freedom and fraternity, with the farmers and the countries that have joined, with the countries from the East that are joining.
Yes, I agree with you.
I believe that we have made great strides with this report.
It was not easy at the beginning and we were rather in a confrontational situation, but we have reached a consensus and that is the most important thing.
This consensus makes it possible to protect the overall project and I believe that this is the European Parliament ’ s great merit.
We have won this bet, and it was not easy, and so I would like to thank the rapporteur, Mr Morganti, and all the Members.
First step.
In this text, we reaffirm the need for equality, justice and fairness between producers and between territories, and I think that this is important.

We have said very clearly, and we have confirmed, the maintenance and development of small farms, the consideration of small farms, which is essential for the quality and biodiversity of the territories.
I think that was also fundamental.
The fact that we have introduced this notion of making agriculture more agronomic, of turning the page on industrial, productivist agriculture, is written into the texts.
We now need to implement this. It is a condition for making agriculture possible for future generations.
This is not an ideological issue.
Finally, today, our concern is that there are indeed threats to the budget.
We will have to respond clearly.

Now what we are waiting for is that we actually enter the second phase, which is clearly the legislative debate, because beyond the texts, beyond the great intentions, it is in the legislative debate that we will really see where we are going, and that is what we are obviously waiting for now.
I would like to end by raising a cry of anger against the attitude of Germany, which has led to food aid to the poorest people in Europe being cut by a factor of five.
It was in the CAP budget.
I want us to find ways of helping the poorest. There are 80 million European citizens who do not eat properly at the end of the day.

Mr President, as much as the European Parliament can boast of a few good advances in economic governance, I would like to invite my colleagues to show some humility, because we are dancing on the edge of a precipice.
Let us be clear about two things.
The first is that the European Parliament has remained silent during the Greek crisis.
Whereas normally we are so quick to react to all sorts of events with resolutions all over the world, this existential crisis seems to have left us speechless.
I am very sorry that we were unable to send a strong, clear, simple message to the Greeks, to the governments of the Member States and to the citizens of Europe.
The second point concerns the scope of this package.
By voting for what I am asking for, these six directives or regulations, we will certainly make the eurozone take a step forward,
more towards a discipline that we have missed for so long, one step, I say, nothing more, which will prove its usefulness in the future.

But we need only look at the heated discussions surrounding the overturning of qualified majority voting to see how far we still have to go, when it should be a matter of rules and automatic sanctions.
The mechanism of the reversed qualified majority continues to act on some as a frightful spectre.
We must, however, remain firm on the principle.
From a strictly political point of view, I regret that the left-wing groups continue to reject the principles of sound budgetary management against all evidence.
Yes, deficits and sustainable growth are incompatible.
Yes, the Member States must also manage their budgets like good family fathers, but no to recovery through public spending. It is a sweet poison in the short term, but it is never a lasting solution.
While the coming weeks are crucial, today we need to rebuild an economic union.
Mr President, this morning, after three years of work, we have voted on a text that is very important for the progress of consumer protection in Europe.
It rightly focuses on internet purchases, which in twenty years might represent twenty-five percent of all commerce in Europe, which is quite considerable.
These purchases will be cross-border in nature.
They will allow consumers to have a wider choice and also to pay much less.
This is real progress.
For SMEs, for all artisans, this is also a very important opportunity to expand their markets.

Among the main advances, I would just like to mention two: the withdrawal period, which is increased to 14 days, and the summary of all the information and the total price to be paid before pressing the final button to place the order.

And finally, I am proud, in the context of these explanations of vote, to say that I, but also all our colleagues, voted unanimously across all political groups, because on such a subject, we did not need polemics, but obviously consensus.
We obtained it.

Madam President, Commissioner, I would like to begin by congratulating our colleague Carlos Coelho on this very difficult file, which required a great deal of technical expertise, a lot of work and a thorough knowledge of the subject.
Controlling our common territory and our European borders is one of the most important challenges we face, and technological tools are more than ever at the service of this challenge.
Indeed, over the past few years, we have seen an unprecedented development in European databases and in the exchange of personal data.
What some people are calling a digital tsunami will certainly be confirmed in the years to come.
The SIS, the VIS and Eurodac are all essential instruments today for ensuring the security of our European citizens.
Ensuring good operational management of these databases is therefore a major issue, to which it is our responsibility to provide an effective response.

This response is of course very delicate, and Carlos Coelho had to face numerous legal, institutional, budgetary, political and technical obstacles, and yet he has managed to produce a balanced text that satisfies all the parties concerned.
The creation of this network agency seems to be the best solution to ensure the internal security of Member States.
No other existing institution or agency could take on the good management of these information systems today.
It will improve the management of existing information systems, offer better synergy between these databases and also allow the integration of instruments that will be adopted in the future.
One of the very delicate issues raised by this dossier was the location of the Agency: where should it be established?
Estonia and France are the only two countries that have put themselves forward as candidates to host it.

France already hosts the Schengen Information System in Strasbourg, for which it has been responsible for operational management and administration since 1995, and consequently has full technical mastery of it.
Following the conclusions of the European Council of December 2003, which gave priority to the new Member States, Estonia also wanted to host this future agency.

I think that the compromise reached is very good. A kind of division of labour has been achieved by transferring the agency ’ s headquarters to Tallinn, where governance, strategy and programme design tasks will be carried out.
The development and operation of the systems will take place in Strasbourg.
The cost-benefit analysis is very balanced, and this co-prime solution does not call into question the centralised management of IT systems.
Mr President, the horizontal social clause and Article 14 of the Treaty of Lisbon guarantee social rights in a binding manner, and in particular the right of access to services of general interest.
It is time to prove to European citizens that the social Europe is under way.

Our social achievements must therefore be given the place they deserve in Community law, by recognising the specific nature of social services of general interest in relation to competition rules, bearing in mind that these services have a strong local base and are based on actors in the social economy.

Mr De Rossa's report is an opportunity to reaffirm the simple but strong and meaningful principles of equality, universality and access to these services organised collectively, which are factors of well-being and social progress.
Commissioner, you said, 'It is time to get down to business '.
Yes, it is time to get down to brass tacks, so I propose that we use this debate to really support a European legislative instrument for social public services.
I therefore invite you to vote for the alternative resolution tabled by the Green Group.
Mr President, debates on genetically modified organisms (GMOs) must be based on irrefutable arguments, both scientifically and legally.
Legally speaking, Member States wishing to restrict or ban GMOs on their territory needed solid and varied arguments.
The rapporteur has broadened the range of margins.
Minus.
The scientific basis must also be irrefutable.
The Council has repeatedly questioned the scientific assessments by EFSA, which are always positive in favour of GMOs, unlike national scientific assessments.

In order to restore confidence, Commissioner, should we not first simply apply the existing legislation, which provides a fair amount of scientific guarantees, particularly in terms of long-term impact assessments?
Why have the Council recommendations of October 2008 remained on paper?
Madam President, Commissioner, first of all, I would like to thank our colleague, Mr Busuttil, for the tremendous work he has done.
These reflections have enriched the initial proposal and we can only congratulate them.
This report gives us an agency with resources multiplied by ten, more in line with the missions entrusted to it.
Recent events have shown us the need for closer cooperation between the European Union, the Member States and the support of a strong agency.
Frontex is finally getting the means to carry out its missions.
By being able to purchase or lease its own equipment and benefit from the services of its own border guards, it becomes more independent and very efficient.
It is also being given new tasks, such as organising voluntary returns and processing personal data, so that we can combat cross-border crime while protecting fundamental rights.

This extension of Frontex 's area of responsibility gives us the means to consider and deal with migration issues through a global approach and not to confine ourselves to a mere mechanical management of borders.
I approve the possibility of exchanging personal data with other European agencies, particularly Europol.
Finally, the human dimension has taken centre stage in this report.
The proposal to set up a consultative council within the Agency guarantees strong recognition of human rights.

I can only welcome this comprehensive proposal, which takes a step forward towards a sound and responsible management of our external borders, in a context where migratory pressures are particularly strong and ongoing.

Mr President, I would like to thank the rapporteur, Mr Mayer, and the remarks made by my colleagues, sometimes critical, but this 2010 activity report is finally a great opportunity to talk about the Committee on Petitions, because it remains too little known.
Yet it is the Commission, which has a direct, sometimes physical, link with the citizen, who can attend our debates in person.
It has undeniable legitimacy in Parliament and a very long-standing expertise, thanks to its direct relationship with citizens.
Indeed, any petitioner can go to Parliament and defend his rights before the Committee on Petitions.
We can also welcome the calmness of the debates, despite the painful cases that are often dealt with.
This committee represents Europe as it should be: listening to its citizens ’ problems and united in finding solutions to protect them.
Unfortunately, our means of action and influence remain limited, particularly in relation to the environmental risks of projects which are sometimes almost completed.
But I also see it when it comes to defending specific cases, such as family rights or children's rights in the event of divorce.
We will, in fact, be carrying out a mission on this subject in November.
I also regret that the national authorities do not always rush to provide us with all the necessary information.
I would like to thank the European Commission for the research and verification work that it carries out, even if sometimes it could go further in the search for the truth.
I must also welcome the constructive work we are doing with the European Ombudsman.
Finally, I appeal to citizens: continue to contact us through petitions, as directly elected representatives.
Our role will always be to safeguard your interests, in strict compliance with European law.
Madam President, Commissioner, I welcome this resolution on homelessness, because it is our responsibility to stress how urgent it is.
First of all, let us note that what was a priority yesterday is even more pressing today.

The lack of a definition and reliable statistical indicators on homelessness cannot prevent us from acknowledging the crisis at hand, namely that the number of people living below the poverty threshold has risen, according to Eurostat, from 79 million in 2009 to 84 million, estimated in 2011.
Ladies and gentlemen, three years ago, our Parliament, concerned with eradicating poverty, asked the Member States to put an end to the problem of homelessness by 2015.
Today, we are ourselves somewhat closer to our goal.
Did the Member States really make the most of 2010, the European Year for Combating Poverty and Social Exclusion, to tackle this great insecurity, the urgency of which has become permanent?
I really do not think so, and I deeply regret it.

To conclude, I would like to stress the need to develop a global, multi-sectoral strategy for homelessness, because, of course, there is poverty and the lack of housing, certainly, but there are also family problems, health issues, various worries or personal vulnerabilities.
We need a global, effective, ambitious, but tailor-made approach that takes account of individuals, of the heterogeneous nature of their life paths and of their life crises.
This is a fundamental right that many of the most vulnerable people are denied.
Madam President, over the past few years, as everyone has said, the European Union has been hit by unprecedented disasters, and to list them here would take up the whole of the debate.
I will mention the explosion at AZF, which we commemorated the tenth anniversary of less than a week ago in Toulouse.
A reinforced reaction at European level, as proposed in Mrs Gardini ’ s report, represents a real opportunity to demonstrate solidarity between our populations.

The Union has been trying for several years to make this pooling of our efforts operational, and it is now time to act together and stop waiting for a new disaster to strike our territories before we learn from it.
Today, we need to make our material, human and financial resources available to everyone in order to prevent new tragedies from occurring.
Madam Commissioner, a moment ago you said that we had to anticipate risks.

It is truly regrettable that, in this context, the European Commission is considering withdrawing from funding the GMES satellite observation programme, which everyone recognises as an essential instrument for the prevention and coordination of responses to disasters.
I urgently call on the Commission to review this position and to support these flagship programmes, which give Europe an undeniable added value.
Madam President, I would like to thank Mrs Gardini for her very good report.
I hope that we will adopt it, and that it will thus pave the way for European solidarity in the service of citizens.
Mr President, Commissioner, Minister, the number of poor people and unemployed people keeps increasing in the European Union.
More than 43 million Europeans are threatened by food poverty.
The time is serious.
Today, six Member States want to put an end to the food aid programme without any replacement solution: Germany, Sweden, Denmark, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands and the Czech Republic.
Of course, there is a decision from the Court of Justice and we must respect it.
However, Commissioner, Parliament called on you last July, on the initiative of the Green Group, and you made relevant proposals to safeguard the programme.
The Greens are ready to work with you on long-term solutions, but as long as millions of people depend on this system, we cannot allow the food aid budget to be cut by four.
The urgency is real.

If nothing is done before the end of the year, more than EUR 300 million will be missing from the food bank, and nothing guarantees that national governments will be able to take over to fill this gap.
This is therefore a real issue of solidarity between peoples.
Let us not allow a minority of irresponsible governments to deprive millions of Europeans of food aid, because this is above all a matter of survival.
The fight against poverty and malnutrition also requires reform of the CAP.
We must fundamentally change the way we produce in order to bring food production closer to populations, particularly the poorest, who also have a right to healthy, balanced food.
Only a truly sustainable CAP will ensure that fewer Europeans need food aid.
The Council must come to its senses and allow those in need to access this vital aid.
I am particularly addressing the future Danish Government, which will be responsible for chairing the European Union from January.
Come to your senses! Do we need riots in Europe to realise that we urgently need to change our approach?

When it comes to saving the banks, the Council has found the solutions, but when it comes to saving the food banks, there is no one left. Parliament said something strong last time: human beings are more important than finance.
It is high time to reaffirm it.
In any case, we will be very vigilant and will not fail to renew our appeal in a constructive manner.
Six Member States hold in their hands the fate of 18 million Europeans in need.
Six Member States which claim to be doing nothing more or less than applying the law.
Law is one thing, humanity and political choices are another.
How can we not express our anger towards those who remain deaf to the alarm cries raised by associations helping the most deprived?

And we, Members of the European Parliament, representatives of all citizens, do we want a Europe where national selfishness determines policies, paying little heed to the nationals of States in financial difficulty?
There is no question of renationalising this aid.

No, let us maintain this programme, give it a stronger legal basis, but let us not abandon those who, in the immediate future, in 2012, are counting on this aid in particularly difficult times of crisis.

Mr President, Commissioner, I am not going to go over the cynicism with which the ArcelorMittal group announced the closure of some of its plants in Europe, and in particular in Belgium, in Liège, which will eventually lead to hundreds of direct redundancies and will jeopardise several thousand indirect jobs.
As a resident of Liège, I share the shock and dismay of the 581 workers who have been dismissed and their families.
But I am also thinking of the severe restructuring announced by the steel group in France, Germany and Luxembourg.
The reasons given are always the same: poor prospects for the development of the European steel market, strong competition from imports from China, Korea,
Russia and Brazil, the shrinking car market and, finally, the competitiveness gap, for example, of the Liège site compared with the best factories in Northern Europe.
In addition, despite a difficult year in 2010, the financial centre of the ArcelorMittal Belgium steel group generated a profit of EUR 1.394 billion.
He paid zero euros in taxes, i.e. a tax rate of zero percent.
It is particularly shocking.
The management of the group is mainly financial, with a stop-go policy being practised at several sites.
The closure of sites is nothing more, in the minds of these managers, than an economic adjustment variable, where human issues are of little importance.
This is no longer acceptable.
You spoke, Commissioner, of rules.
Is it not time that the Commission drew up a binding code of corporate social responsibility for multinational companies?

Six years ago, our Parliament adopted a report on offshoring, on corporate codes of conduct, on the repayment of subsidies, on mandatory consultation between companies, trade unions and public authorities.
Since then, we have to admit that little concrete progress has been made on these various proposals.
So what is the Commission going to do about this and within what time frame?
If we need to support the competitiveness of enterprises and make Europe attractive in terms of economic investment, we also need to adopt a legislative framework
strong European Union to deal with relocation and restructuring, and to ensure that another form of capital, human capital, is fully taken into account.

Finally, in the case of the Liège site, I hope that it will be able to benefit from the intervention of the Globalisation Adjustment Fund in order to compensate the workers who are being made redundant.

Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, the wave of redundancies hitting workers across Europe should make us reflect on the role that we, as political leaders, have to play in relation to multinational companies that act

without any social ethics, which carry out stock market layoffs, relocate to reduce costs, increase shareholder profits, and please the rating agencies, while ignoring social and environmental standards.

In its resolution of 20 September on the future of the Globalisation Adjustment Fund, Parliament clearly expressed its desire to support, above all, workers affected by the negative effects of restructuring.
However, we also asked the Commission to ensure consistency between the EIB ’ s actions and the EU ’ s industrial policy, particularly through the use of the Structural Funds.

We also insisted that the future FEM regulation include improvements so that the fund does not produce the random, moral effects it produces today on multinational companies, as is the case with ArcelorMittal and Nokia.
Commissioner, we can no longer look at the Structural Funds and the Globalisation Adjustment Fund separately.
take into account the restructuring strategies of companies that benefit from them, without examining the cost-benefit ratio of a policy, I repeat, very
Mr President, the European Ombudsman is doing a brilliant job investigating complaints against the institutions and bodies of the European Union.

This instrument is both necessary to facilitate free movement, that of our businesses and of our citizens, but it is also a tremendous guarantee, particularly in the face of the Union's detractors, who too often say that the European Union is nothing but a bureaucratic machine without control.
That is clearly nonsense. Europe is doing real work on itself, thanks in particular to the European Ombudsman.
However, let me relay a point that I deeply regret, in which the Commission is under fire.
Firstly, there are the figures: 65% is the real figure, not 12%, of the inquiries opened by the Ombudsman concerning the European Commission.

There are also the players. Many companies come to see us to explain their incomprehension of the Commission, of a European Commission that remains deaf to their requests, particularly with regard to access to documents. Likewise, in terms of transparency in decision-making.
Few people can make sense of it.
And finally, from personal experience, I admit that I have sometimes been shocked by the reactions of certain services of the European Commission present in the Committee on Petitions.
As you know, the European Commission gives us its opinion on petitions, an opinion that we always take into account and which is necessary.

However, it occasionally happens that we see representatives of the European Commission refusing to answer questions from Members of the European Parliament, or hiding behind a wooden leg almost as well as politicians, and this in front of citizens pleading their case.

I would therefore like to remind you that Europe must have a human face, that behind the administrative problems that the Ombudsman deals with and resolves, there are Europeans who are relieved to be listened to, but disappointed by these
behaviour. Citizens are then negatively affected by their confrontation with the institutions, creating perhaps a sense of revolt, incomprehension and, in the long term, perhaps Euroscepticism.
I sincerely hope that these criticisms and those of the Ombudsman against the institutions in general will be heard. Thank you.
Madam President, professional mobility and growth, professional mobility and the competitiveness of our European Union: these are the terms in which today ’ s subject is framed.
So we, the PPE Group, have supported the report by our colleague Mrs McClarkin, to the drafting of which we have greatly contributed, and we obviously support the

main recommendation of this report for more professional mobility, more simplification and efficiency in the procedures for recognition of professional qualifications, while guaranteeing the safety of patients, the safety of consumers.
But it is in terms of the path towards growth that the issue of increasing professional mobility arises.
And indeed, the forum for a single market, which took place in Krakow at the beginning of October, did not make a mistake.

which reminded us that Member States must recognise the potential of the internal market to stimulate growth and job creation, and must act to remove all obstacles to the completion of the internal market.
Likewise, the European Council of 23 October last also set as one of its essential priorities to achieve growth, and I quote, 'smart, sustainable, and inclusive'.

inclusive, the implementation of the twelve priority proposals of the Single Market Act, the twelve proposals made by the Commission, under which the recognition of professional qualifications is included.

We can clearly see that it is by increasing professional mobility that we can hope to release the growth potential of the internal market, our internal market of 500 million inhabitants, of 500 million consumers.

Professional mobility must therefore not only ensure, if we strengthen it, if we modernise it, if we improve it, the free movement of our compatriots within this market, but also, we hope, allow us to achieve additional growth margins.
That is why we have particularly, and I would like to stress one practical recommendation, supported the implementation of a voluntary professional card.
Madam President, I would like to begin by congratulating the rapporteur, Mr Kaczmarek, on his excellent work.
I would like to say that it is important that each year the European Parliament draw up an assessment of the work of the ACP-EU Joint Parliamentary Assembly, inasmuch as
This Assembly plays a decisive role in strengthening North-South dialogue and in establishing a development policy based on partnership and exchange.
2010 was a busy year for the ACP, which met twice, as my predecessors said, in Spain and in the DRC, to exchange views, in particular
on the impact of climate change in ACP countries, the impact of the global crisis, or again on media freedom and independence and food security.
But in 2010, the ACP also sent its first election observation mission for the elections to this Parliament.
I had the honour of co-chairing this mission to Burundi for the presidential elections with my colleague, Mr Assarid, Member from Mali.
This election observation mission was a real success.

We were able to meet all the political stakeholders concerned and, thanks to the dual origin of this delegation, which was both ACP and European, we embodied a different legitimacy from European or African missions.
I therefore invite the ACP Bureau to repeat this experience, which allows us to put into practice the solidarity and privileged relations that unite the European Union to the ACP countries.
I would like to thank Mr. Dardenne for his report.
Certainly, the crisis is economic and financial, but for our fellow citizens it is above all a social crisis.
Reducing the number of people in precarious situations by 20 million by 2020 requires strong measures involving the various levels of public authority.

We must, more than ever, keep the commitments made by the European Union in 2010, during the European Year for Combating Poverty and Social Exclusion and during the creation of the European Platform against Poverty and Social Exclusion, in order to have a European framework for social and territorial cohesion.

We must make European funds permanent and optimise them, and in particular the programme to assist the poorest, which is currently maintained, improve social protection and support local or national initiatives that develop services for the care of children, older people and dependent people.
Poverty is a scourge that must be fought horizontally, and it is now time to act to allow the most deprived to escape their precarious situation.

And we must, as political leaders at European, national and local level, take the necessary measures to ensure them, without discrimination, access to housing, access to healthcare, access to justice, access to education, access to a first job with a minimum income, perhaps indexed according to the poverty threshold of each Member State.
Investing in the promotion of innovation and social experimentation and in reducing poverty will allow us to implement a strategy for growth that is sustainable, smart and inclusive.
The Europe we want and need today is a Europe based on solidarity.
Mr President, I would like to begin by congratulating the rapporteur, Mrs Levin, on her excellent work.

As rapporteur for the opinion of the development committee, I am pleased that the committee on fisheries has taken up the elements contained in our opinion, because it is crucial that the development dimension
be taken into account in measures aimed at combating illegal fishing worldwide, in the interests of policy coherence and close links with development policy.
Indeed, combating illegal fishing requires global solutions and the involvement of all States, including developing countries.
However, the technical and financial capacities of the States must also be taken into account in the actions to be undertaken.
Indeed, we cannot impose the same constraints without support measures on developed countries and developing countries.
Small island states, for example, have very limited capabilities.
We must therefore support them in their efforts to combat illegal fishing.
That is why the Union should increase the financial envelope for the fisheries sector when it concludes agreements in this area with developing countries.

Furthermore, it is crucial that measures to combat illegal fishing be implemented, as this practice undermines the development of countries by reducing the financial resources that these countries could derive from their fishing economic activity.
Yet these resources are necessary for developing countries to implement social, economic and education policies.

Finally, it is important that the European Union promote, when implementing these measures to combat illegal fishing in developing countries, the involvement of civil society and businesses alongside the beneficiary States.
A holistic approach is necessary to curb this scourge that is illegal fishing. Thank you.
Mr President, I would like to say that, largely thanks to these outermost regions, the European Union is the world ’ s leading maritime power.
This is a major asset for Europe, and the development and implementation of a major policy on the seas, oceans and seabed are essential for the future.
But I am afraid that EUR 20 or 50 million will not be sufficient to meet this major challenge.

The fight against illegal fishing is part of this major policy, and it is urgent at European level to find an adequate solution to this problem, particularly in the context of international cooperation.
I would also like to point out that the maritime zones of Guadeloupe, French Guiana, Martinique, Mayotte and Réunion are regularly violated and plundered.
As the report does not mention it, I take this opportunity to draw my colleagues ' and the Commission ' s attention to this particular issue.
Madam President, I would like to begin by congratulating the Polish Presidency, which has shown dynamism and determination.
Next week at the Council, our Heads of State and Government will have to take essential decisions for the future of the eurozone, for the future of the Union.
They will also have to seriously address the issue of recovery and growth.

That said, we can clearly see that today financial stability has become the top priority, and that the pressures and tensions exerted from outside on the countries of the eurozone, but also within the eurozone, are destroying our credibility and undermining confidence.
And in order to hope for the return of confidence, we must restore financial stability, break the terrible progression of national deficits and sovereign debts.
Financial stability has become the top priority, it has become an urgent obligation for all.

Our democracies have all committed themselves to very courageous programmes of cleaning up their public finances, reducing their debt. But financial stability is also a prerequisite for any recovery of growth.
But anyone who talks about solidarity, financial stability and budgetary discipline, because they go hand in hand, of course, must also talk about solidarity.
It seems important to us to ensure this double movement of financial stability, budgetary discipline and solidarity.
A final word on democracy.
Be careful not to delegitimize our democratic institutions.

rather than thinking about resorting to a legal mechanism, I would say, to sanction in the case of budgetary excesses, let us rather think about mechanisms of control and sanctions exercised by democratic institutions.

To be completely frank, I am not the spokesperson for the French Minister of Foreign Affairs. And quite frankly, even though I belong to the same political party as him, I am not permanently, I would say, in a position to relay the statements of the French Minister of Foreign Affairs.
To be quite frank once again, I am not aware of any recent statements by Alain Juppé to that effect.
I simply think that France's current position is to affirm and constantly reaffirm that the euro is truly the heart of our European integration, that safeguarding the euro means

the safeguarding of this common project and the safeguarding of the European Union. And in this movement to safeguard the euro, we must of course think about the future, think about protecting future generations and preserving peace on our continent.
Mr President, colleagues, tomorrow we will vote for the third time on the new cooperation instrument.
development, which provides for support measures in the banana sector for ACP banana-exporting countries following the reduction of their tariff preferences, the BAM.
This aid programme has a budget of EUR 190 million and will benefit ten ACP countries until 2013.
It is of crucial importance for our African and Caribbean partners, inasmuch as the banana sector has considerable economic and social weight in these countries.
I deeply regret that the Parliament ’ s demands regarding the application of delegated acts have not been taken up in this instrument.
We have nevertheless managed to obtain some progress.
Indeed, the text provides clear objectives and precise criteria for allocating resources to the Commission.
The Commission has also made a statement specifying the methodology used.
Moreover, we have a Council statement on the next multiannual financial framework, stating that the latter would duly take into account delegated acts in the external aid instruments after 2013.
Finally, in its resolution, Parliament specifies – and this point seems crucial to me – that this is in no way a precedent.

And I would like to tell you that I come from Togo, where the 22nd session of the ACP-EU was held, and I believe I can tell you that our ACP friends are counting on us, given the urgency of the situation.
The funds should have been released two years ago.
We must be aware of the deplorable consequences for our ACP partners if the ICD BAM were to be rejected.
The situation of certain farmers is dramatic. We cannot wait any longer and must act accordingly.
I therefore count on you to support the text. Thank you.
Mr President, our coordinator, Mr Berman, cannot finally be present this evening for personal reasons.
However, I know that he completely shares my analysis on this matter.

The conciliation on this legislative package was long and tedious, and while the result on the human rights instruments and ICI Plus is satisfactory, we are very far from the mark on the banana instrument.

Thus, the compromise reached on this text sweeps aside the mandate given to our negotiating team, which was to place Parliament on an equal footing with the Council on all programming decisions concerning the choice of strategic priorities and financial allocations.
Because it is indeed the powers of our Assembly and the very spirit of the Treaty of Lisbon that are at stake here.
The choice of programming priorities is a highly political decision on which our Assembly must have its say.
By categorically refusing to recognise any form of parliamentary control and by resorting to shameful blackmail, the Council has once again shown an unacceptable ideological retreat.
But the Member States must understand that the EU's external action is henceforth a shared competence between our institutions.
That is why our Parliament must remain sovereign and reject the proposal on the Baden instrument.
This is the only way we have of showing the Council and the Commission that Parliament wants more transparency in Community decisions.
If we lose this battle, the absurd would be that we should wait until we have a new treaty to finally obtain the powers that Lisbon already provides.
Mr President, I am probably going to shock the more federalist among you, but I think that a defence policy is an eminently national policy.
It meets the strategic, diplomatic and security needs specific to each nation.
Sharing capabilities is nothing more than a loss of sovereignty. The single market in military equipment is a big joke, when some Member States, believing that their
The defence guarantee to Washington is to equip itself primarily with American equipment, Europe being incapable, in this area as in so many others, of imposing Community preference.
As regards the common security challenges facing the Member States of the Union, there is nothing that cannot be resolved by intergovernmental cooperation, without any intervention from Brussels.

I am sorry, but entrusting these areas to Mrs Ashton would be tantamount to acknowledging that Moody's and Standard & Poor's have the legitimacy to judge our economic policies, and to accept that the Merkel-Sarkozy duo could impose a German Europe on 27 peoples: a pure mental aberration.

For the past 30 years, and even more so since the disappearance of the Soviet threat, defence budgets have been the first to be sacrificed on the altar of budgetary austerity, to the point of rendering most of our military tools

deficient or decrepit, and all in the name of purely financial considerations, with each one waiting for the neighbour or the supposed big American brother to make the efforts that he is no longer prepared to make himself.
But you are barking up the wrong tree.

It is only by returning to independent and resolute national policies that we can individually equip ourselves with the necessary means, not only for our security and national interests, but also to cooperate in these means to meet common challenges.
An addition of weaknesses and gaps has never made a strength.

Mr President, I wanted to express the support of the French delegation of the Group of the European People's Party (Christian Democrats) for the treaty on European economic stability, coordination and governance, and for the main conclusions reached by the Council.
Let us not be mistaken.
This treaty represents an important step towards greater economic and budgetary integration, towards greater financial stability.
Let us say it again here.
There can be no lasting recovery of growth and employment without the sanitisation of our public finances.
Of course, growth cannot be decreed. It is not a treaty that alone can revive growth.
But a treaty, that is to say a set of rules and mutual commitments, can provide confidence and accountability.

And as the President of the Socialist Group thought it good to introduce some politician's politics into this chamber, and by referring to the upcoming French presidential campaign, mentioning the Socialist candidate in this French presidential election, I wanted to respond.
We, the EPP, consider the statements made by the French socialist candidate, who said that he wanted to renegotiate the treaty concluded by 25, to be totally irresponsible.
Negotiate what and with whom?
These words are totally irresponsible.
They pay little heed to the word of the French State, to the respect of the French State's commitments.

Madam Ashton, Madagascar is one of the poorest countries in the world and is going through a political crisis whose outcome will depend on the implementation of the roadmap, leading up to presidential and legislative elections.
This country ' s growth is negative.
Normally, our contribution is around 50% of this country's budget.
The European Union has rightly suspended its budgetary support to the ninth and tenth European Development Funds, but not to the OMD.
Meanwhile, the population is suffering and companies based in Madagascar are sometimes forced to contribute against their will.

While supporting your position, we would like you to use all your weight with the SADC so that the legal and judicial confusion concerning the return of the former president is settled as soon as possible, as it is one of the most sensitive elements of the implementation of this roadmap.

I would like your opinion on the matter, and I also inform you that the EU-Madagascar friendship group, which we have just created in the European Parliament, is available to work with you and the Commission in the interests of the Malagasy people and in the defence of the values of our Union.
Mr President, I would like to thank the rapporteur, Mr Iturgaiz, for the quality of his work, for the long negotiation process that he had to lead and for the patience that he showed.

We are finally arriving at the vote on this report, which is extremely important, because it enables us to bring back to this House, to the Commission and to the Council, the difficulties and fears of our fellow citizens.
This is further proof, if any were needed, of the effectiveness of the right to petition in Europe.
Petitions on the environment are the most numerous, and waste is a major concern for our fellow citizens.
This issue must respond to a societal problem.
We are consuming at full speed.
We are only now realising that what we do not consume, we throw away.
This awareness must be strengthened among citizens, that much is clear.
But this report is also a wake-up call for the Member States.
It is still worrying to see that the Member States seem to have no control over the situation.
Only six Member States have transposed the Waste Framework Directive on time.
The number of complaints received on this subject sometimes demonstrates a lack of transparency in the choice of landfill sites.
Finally, and this is the most worrying thing, organised crime seems to be doing as it pleases.
A market worth EUR 75 billion is bound to attract interest.
The Member States must send a strong signal.
We need to increase sanctions against companies that misuse public contracts for personal gain.
This report is a wake-up call to the Member States, but the European Commission must also play its part and check that waste directives are properly implemented.
We need to make this file visible, but also credible.
If no one plays ball, we will continue to waste time and money on verification and re-verification procedures that could last for years.
Let us listen to the message from the petitioners and put an end to this fiasco as soon as possible.
Mr President, I would like to begin by congratulating Mr Nicholson on the courage he has shown in the face of the Council and the Commission.
Nevertheless, I fear that what we have achieved will not allow for a balance between producers and industry.
This agreement reminds me of that illusion that children tell about a paradise where vegetarian lions would eat grass alongside sheep.
I do not believe it for a second. Since 2003, Mrs Fischer Boel has done everything she can to ideologically destroy the milk quota system.
In October 2009, the Court of Auditors nevertheless acknowledged the effectiveness of these milk quotas.

Today, we are in a situation that is going to become even more dramatic, and we can see what is currently happening in Denmark, where the most solid farms, with increased production, are all going bankrupt, with a 40% drop in land prices.
Do we want that at European level?
I say no: we need to control production with the participation of the public authorities.
Madam President, Commissioner, the standards for protecting personal data in third countries still differ considerably from European standards.
It is, however, our duty to ensure that the European minimum protection standards that we uphold are respected and guaranteed to European citizens.
It is essential that personal data be processed fairly and collected for specified and legitimate purposes.
European citizens must be able to guarantee their right of access and rectification of personal data concerning them, as well as to lodge a legal appeal in the event of violation of their rights.
We must act to allow European and international standards to coexist and to resolve a legal conflict that could potentially harm European citizens.
European legislation alone is not enough.
It must intelligently coordinate and coexist with international standards, as we cannot shirk our duties and commitments to European citizens.
This situation echoes other debates in this Chamber.
It reminds us of the relevance of projects such as the implementation of a European programme to monitor terrorist financing.
Such a system would give us a tool that has proved its worth in the United States, while fully respecting internal law and therefore the protection of European citizens ’ personal data.
European institutions must continue their commitment in this respect and fully assume their protection standards. Thank you.
Mr President, first of all, I would like to thank the rapporteurs for their work.

In response to the crisis, the European Union has just spent over a year strengthening its economic governance to avoid macroeconomic imbalances and excessive budgetary deficits threatening the European economy, but this has not prevented social exclusion or the rise in unemployment.
It is high time – no, it is now urgent – that the objectives of the Europe 2020 strategy become an absolute priority for the European Commission and for the Member States, in a growth perspective.

Each Member State will have to draw up a national employment plan, which means that so many different subjects will be highlighted, such as training, retraining, micro-credit, apprenticeships, paid internships, etc.
As Mr Andor reminded us earlier, we have 23 million unemployed people in Europe, but also 23 million SMEs and EUR 23 billion of European funds dedicated to employment that are unused.
We could almost make it a 23-23 strategy.
And that is without counting the EUR 60 billion of cohesion funds that have not been allocated.
In Denmark and the Netherlands, social initiatives have been taken and have proved their effectiveness, based on flexicurity.
Let us take inspiration from it.

This also means that our social policy as a whole must be better adapted, with more advantageous social incentives than those linked to unemployment, and active support for all socially responsible businesses.
We had objectives to reduce poverty to be achieved by 2020, but we already know that they will not be met, due to a lack of strong and adequate measures taken by the Member States.

Our role as MEPs must therefore be fully exercised, and it is our responsibility to demand that the same energy used to take strong economic governance measures should also be used for ambitious, effective and binding social governance measures.
Madam President, the cause of women undoubtedly owes a great deal to Europe.
The principle of equality between men and women was already enshrined in the Treaty of Rome and has been constantly reaffirmed since then in the various treaties.
Gender equality is truly a fundamental value of our European project.
Consequently, Europe has always worked very actively for gender equality and for the advancement of women.
Progress has been made little by little.
Many legislative instruments have been adopted, have been put in place, directives.
Here in Parliament itself, for many years now, we have had a committee on women's rights and gender equality that is extremely active and vigilant.
An European Institute for Gender Equality has been created.
Programmes are put in place, financial resources are voted year after year.
More recently, on March 2, we had the opportunity to celebrate the second edition of the European Day for Equal Pay, the Equal Pay Day.
European Equal Pay Day. So, undoubtedly, the legislative arsenal exists, the European will has been materialized on several occasions.
Yet we must admit that the fight is still very much alive, the fight for equality between men and women, and particularly for equal pay.
There is still, on average, across all European Union countries, a wage gap of around 17%.
At equal work, women still earn 17% less than men.
This is, of course, quite unacceptable.

The Women's Rights and Gender Equality Committee is currently working on a draft resolution calling on the European Commission to revise the 2006 directive on equal treatment between men and women in the workplace.
which is one of the most worrying fights and one of the most central concerns and demands of European women in all our societies.

On another subject, that of the establishment of quotas, quotas both within the decision-making bodies of large companies and within political life, I come from a country, France, which adopted a long time ago, more than
10 years ago, binding rules on quotas in politics, and more recently, about a year ago, a law on quotas in the boards of directors of large companies.
France is not the only country, of course, to have opted for such legislation.
So, what is the balance sheet after a few years of application in one case, a few months in the other? We must admit that things have changed, that things have moved forward.
So, a necessary evil.
Quotas have had an effect.

Madam Commissioner, you recently took an important step towards the introduction of quotas at European level in the decision-making bodies of large companies by launching this major public consultation aimed at collecting opinions on the actions to be taken, the potentially legislative actions to be taken.
On this subject, you will have my full and complete support. Thank you.
Mr President, Commissioner, on the occasion of this resolution, allow me to address two subjects. First, the issue of water management in the world and in Europe.
Europe, and then a question to the European Commission, so that acts can be adopted to respond to a number of our concerns linked to the common agricultural policy.
As we know, the issue is that water is the big issue of the twenty-first century.
It is because we will have answers that we will have peace in the world and in Europe.
We know what is at stake in terms of management, we know what is at stake in terms of food security.

And on the occasion of this forum, which, as our colleague Rivasi reminded us, has too many purely commercial concerns at its heart, the discussions that have taken place in recent days in Marseille have been very revealing.
One question: why is there no coherence between water management and water-related resources and the common agricultural policy?
Nothing in the CAP reform is indicated regarding this management, as our colleague Mr Sarbaux said earlier.
So let us have answers.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to begin by thanking the rapporteur and our assistants for their work.
This report now truly lives up to its name, which is to remove obstacles to citizenship.
Europe is a wonderful project, but we too often notice that it is not yet perfectly defined.
Take the economic crisis, for example.
It has exposed our weaknesses and allowed us to build more solidly the institutions for regulating our single market.
For the European citizen, it is the same.
The Committee on Petitions, a real complaints office, records the grievances of our fellow citizens every day.
In three days, the European Citizens' Initiative will complete this system.
Despite all our policies calling for mobility, for adherence to European values, for exchanges, there are still far too many obstacles to the exercise of citizenship.
And a citizen who does not feel European is a citizen who will not vote, a citizen who will not see the interest that Europe represents in his or her everyday life.
With my colleagues from the EPP Group, we have made a huge number of proposals, the most numerous, and I would like to thank them for that.
This is a report that must create a sense of belonging and European identity that we must recreate and maintain through these measures.
Our Europe must be a Europe that protects.
Commissioner Reding has made 25 very concrete proposals in favour of citizens.
They are essential to re-establishing trust between the citizen and Europe.
But the proposals contained in this report reflect other expectations of our fellow citizens: online purchases, moving house, children's rights, employment,
citizen participation, healthcare abroad, twinning, minority rights, etc. We still have too much difficulty in fully benefiting from the opportunities offered by Europe.
Rights must be exercised, claims must be heard.
We are here the relay.
The European Year of Citizens in 2013 should show citizens that, with free movement, there is also free use of rights in Europe.
I believe that taxation is an interesting tool for changing behaviours.

I believe that energy consumption should be regulated with an indicative price signal for the consumer, and, above all, I think that, in order to avoid any distortion on the internal market, we must try to bring our fiscal practices closer together.
The principle of proportionality of the different levels of taxation of energy products therefore seems to me to be a necessity.
In the case we are dealing with, for example, this would put an end to the diesel tourism we are currently witnessing, which is not acceptable.
I therefore share the philosophy of the Commission proposal to revise the directive.

Nevertheless, if we are to achieve our objectives in terms of reducing CO emissions or improving energy efficiency, our collective decisions must not penalise citizens or sectors particularly exposed to international competition.

price volatility on world markets, and I am thinking, for example, of the agricultural sector. The proposed directive will certainly have a different impact depending on the level of taxation in each Member State, but it will have an impact on diesel prices.
The question is: who will ultimately have to bear this price increase?
Are there any real, practicable, realistic, consumer-accessible alternatives?
As shadow rapporteur in the Committee on the Environment, I support an ambitious but above all sustainable position for all.

I therefore plead for greater progressivity and for the proposal and its principle of proportionality to come into force in 2030, provided that a new impact assessment is carried out, updating the one on which the Commission is basing its work, which was carried out five years ago.

A few months ago, here, we had a debate on the wave of redundancies in Europe, as a result of the economic crisis, particularly at Arcelor, a multinational present in nine Member States, which is restructuring its production facilities in Europe in the face of a decline in demand for steel.
Since the closure of two blast furnaces in Belgium, the one in Luxembourg and the shutdown of blast furnaces in France, it is estimated that nearly 6 000 direct jobs will be threatened in 2012.

Beyond unacceptable practices such as 'stop-go' practices, which we should be able to better regulate, it is the more general issue of European steelmaking and adaptation to change that urgently needs answers.

Six months ago, the Commission told us that, in the light of the economic crisis, we needed to carry out a new consultation exercise on responsible restructuring with the social partners and public authorities.
My question is simple: six months later, where do we stand?

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, I would like to put a stop to the unfounded allegations that between 500 000 and one million fake biometric passports are circulating in our country.
France takes very seriously the risks of fraud relating to identity documents.
It does not take lightly to offences in this area.
At the initiative of his government, identity theft is a criminal offence punishable by one year in prison and a fine of EUR 15 000.
The conditions for issuing identity documents have been simplified, enabling the prefectures to free up additional resources to combat this fraud.
Thus, each prefecture has a fraud officer.
Furthermore, the security measures for issuing identity documents have been strengthened.
For example, civil status data provided could be verified between administrative services, as far as possible electronically, in order to limit the risk of falsification.
Some birth certificates are also issued on security paper.
The same strictness applies to proof of residence.
The Ministry of the Interior has committed itself to increasing the security of proof of address documents by inserting a barcode.

The standard processing of a request for a document includes the authorised consultation of relevant databases, the comparison of all documents submitted in previous requests for documents and the possible authentication of supporting documents.

The security of the issue of the documents is further guaranteed by the double appearance of the applicant, first when the application is submitted and then when the document is collected, as well as by the taking of two digital fingerprints.
Not to mention the enhanced checks for the renewal of lost or stolen documents for comparative purposes with the data kept by the prefectures.
Furthermore, France notifies international cooperation bodies, and particularly Interpol, about passports issued, lost or stolen, so that they are not used for fraudulent purposes.
Ladies and gentlemen, France, like the rest of the European Union, is committed.
Mr President, Commissioner, first of all, a statement: our fellow citizens increasingly want to find and consume locally produced, seasonal food, for health and quality reasons in particular.
We must find ways to enable farmers to respond effectively and quickly to this growing demand.

We must support the creation of local infrastructure for producers, such as the maintenance of local slaughterhouses, which, while guaranteeing strict sanitary control, allow local supply chains to be developed and direct sales to be strengthened.
Support for local agriculture and direct sales should help producers market their products and make consumers aware of them.
Strengthening these direct relationships will allow farmers to keep a greater share of the added value created on their farms.

We need to encourage and facilitate direct sales on the farm and support collective actions by farmers who wish to open collective sales points, which would offer local consumers a wider range of products.

The European Commission must facilitate the emergence, sustainability and development of direct sales systems, by delivery to consumers, such as AMAPs in France, which allow nearly 3 000 farmers to supply 60 000 families.

Finally, Member States should be able to add other criteria, particularly concerning the size of the farm, the volume of production or the traditional nature of the product, in order to define local farming.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I welcome the fact that this amending budget on the financing of ITER was adopted by a large majority in the Committee on Budgets on 29 March.

By funding this extra cost, we will avoid throwing away a strategic project for fundamental research in which the European Union plays a leading role, and we will honour our international commitments.
Let us recall that none of the seven Euratom Community partners has questioned its overall contribution to the project.
Japan, despite the Fukushima disaster, has decided to scrupulously respect its budgetary commitments to ITER.
Without this agreement, hundreds of researchers would have had to suspend their work and the ITER project would have suffered damaging delays.
Today, a thousand people work for ITER in Cadarache.
From 2015 to 2017 or 2018, the construction phase of the buildings and the assembly of the machine could involve up to 4 000 or 5 000 people on this site.
To conclude, Mr President, ITER is a source of pride for Europe and a symbol of our excellence.
Mr President, Commissioner, rapporteur, thank you for the work we have done together.
The European Union needs a manifesto, a common political vision on the major challenges linked to the environment, by 2020 and beyond.
That is the whole point of launching a seventh environmental action programme, by combining the existing financial frameworks and mobilising new financial resources.
This programme must guarantee coherence and coordination between the European Union ’ s environmental strategies and sectoral policies, whether agriculture,
fishing, research and innovation, health, transport, regional development, land use planning and urban policies, of course.
Despite the economic crisis which is afflicting the European Union, we must not give in to short-term thinking and abandon all ambition in the environmental and climate fields.
Inaction would be fatal.
The stakes are too high, particularly in terms of the efficient use of our resources and the enhancement of natural capital.
We must therefore resolutely invest in the sustainable economy and eco-innovation, sources of growth, competitiveness and employment.
It is up to us to promote eco-design, recycling, but also environmental labelling of products, in order to avoid waste and poor consumer choices.
We cannot ignore this essential environmental education for European citizens.
The environmental action programme is a tool for political planning, but it is also a communication tool.
It must provide the clarity and predictability needed by national and local authorities, by citizens, but also by entrepreneurs and investors, on the European Union ’ s environmental choices.
European regulation should not become a punishment or a straitjacket.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, in the face of the profound changes to which our world is subject today, the EU must reform its system of generalised preferences.
Like any compromise, this one is no exception to the rule.
While it is satisfactory from a strictly commercial point of view, it is far from perfect, and we must bear in mind that this regulation is aimed at developing countries.
By developing its new paradigm of differentiation, the Commission says it wants to refocus its action in favour of the poorest countries.
This seems entirely reasonable at first sight.
To this end, the Commission has nevertheless developed a strictly accounting vision based on the sole criterion of GDP per inhabitant.
We can clearly see the limits of this approach.
What about the many middle-income developing countries whose economies are not diversified and which are vulnerable to climate variability?
The compromise reached with the Council does not answer this question, and that is why I am calling on colleagues to vote in favour of the three amendments that will be tabled on this text.
Mr President, Commissioner, the problem of the availability of public consultations in the different languages of the Union is not new, unfortunately.
This is a crucial issue, in my opinion, on which I have not only submitted several written questions to the European Commission since 2008, but have also personally questioned Mr Barroso in writing.

Indeed, the Commission must comply with the principle of equal access to training for all European citizens, as the Court of First Instance of the European Communities reminded us in its judgment of 20 November 2008.
I was not at all satisfied with the European Commission ’ s response, according to which public consultations do not claim to provide a totally representative picture of all opinions.
According to this answer, the Commission takes into account the fact that certain positions and views may be over-represented to the detriment of others.
That is not an answer.
I am wondering about the criteria that would make it possible to measure an over-representation of the European population.
If written consultations are not representative because they are only available in English, for example, they fail in their purpose of consulting European citizens.
The result therefore gives a misleading indication and should not be used by the European Commission.
If we want to keep written consultations as a tool at the basis of the European Commission's legislative proposals, we must guarantee that they are democratic, transparent and fair for all citizens.

If the European Commission conducts written consultations, it must take its responsibilities seriously and ensure that the necessary resources are available for multilingualism and the translation of questionnaires.
To conclude, I would like to add that multilingualism is not the only obstacle.

The analysis should also take into account the very short deadlines for submitting contributions, as well as the Commission's limited resources for communicating these consultations, which are, unfortunately, accessible mainly to the initiated.
The proposed regulation aims to give the European Commission greater powers to control national budgets and economic policies.
The rules will be stricter and the defaulting states will have budget cuts and structural reforms imposed on them by the technocrats in Brussels in order to re-establish their financial balance.
This enhanced surveillance is called guardianship.
States will be punished, fined, spanked, one might say. Financial penalties will be strengthened and made more automatic.
Even if certain amendments aim to prevent budget austerity measures by introducing growth measures, your proposals are there only to make the pill easier to swallow.
Growth, as you and I know, cannot be decreed.
Without consulting the peoples and with the complicity of Brussels, we are definitively losing our budgetary sovereignty, our freedom to choose our policies,

And when, little by little, according to the tactics of the Chinese thief, the European bureaucratic, technocratic and totalitarian State has finished forming itself, we will have lost our freedom as a people, period.
Mr President, without underestimating the importance of the economic choices we have to make, you will allow me to make a more political comment in the context of this debate.
In recent weeks, we have again seen evidence of the double talk of certain great European leaders or those who think they are.
whereas everyone is calling for coordination of economic, budgetary and fiscal policies that is finally worthy of the name, and this requirement even appears to be a condition
of the survival of the eurozone, now that they are well established at home, these gentlemen think it clever to denounce the balanced recommendations emanating from the
the Commission from fulfilling certain commitments or, in the worst case scenario, to turn public opinion against these ' Brussellos ', as they say.
This irresponsible attitude would be laughable if we were not in such a tragic situation.
I am telling you quite frankly that our debates on stability, the necessary consolidation of public finances or the efforts to be made in terms of growth are clearly doomed to fail.
failure if the past mistakes in terms of debt and public deficits do not come to an end and if the governments of the Member States continue in their ineptitude.
We are playing with fire at the moment.
European discipline must apply to everyone, without exception, even to the Grand Duchy of Luxembourg.
This requires a minimum of courage, and our public opinion is ready for it, much more so than some of their leaders would like to believe.
And a last word to Mrs. Goulard, otherwise I have to show her the blue card. I would say to Mrs. Goulard, in redemption there is ransom.

Mr President, I should like to begin by commending the determination of the European Commission and, in particular, yours, Commissioner, to have tirelessly placed the recovery and deepening of the internal market at the top of the European agenda.

You mentioned it, Commissioner. For more than two years now, both the Commission and the European Parliament have been working to identify the missing links in the internal market, to identify the challenges and the sectors where we need more internal market, more internal market for more growth, more competitiveness, more jobs.

The European Council, for its part, also concluded, albeit later, by underlining the importance it attached to the swift and effective implementation of the twelve priority proposals for the internal market, the twelve growth levers.
Instead of constantly trying to invent new instruments for growth, we would do well to work with the toolbox we already have.

We are therefore taking the opportunity of the resolution to be put to the vote in our Assembly tomorrow to send a strong political message to the Member States: more speed and efficiency in transposing directives already
