adopted. More mobilisation and commitment in the newly opened projects. I will mention just one, that of enhancing the mobility of workers, more ease of movement, more fluidity of movement.
Commissioner, we will welcome with great interest the second stage of the Single Market Act that you will present after the summer.
We will need to show both political will and the ability to put pressure on the Member States.
We experienced the Mediator scandal a while ago.
We now have the breast implant scandal, which, unfortunately, as you said, Commissioner, highlights the dysfunctions of European health safety.
This scandal of breast implants, these 14 years of fraud, 1996-2010, unpunished.
There are 500 000 prostheses in use worldwide, 8 000 implant withdrawals and 48 confirmed cases of cancer.
Finally, 3 500 complaints have been filed. This figure is shocking.

How, despite the warnings from the US Food and Drug Administration, Commissioner, and the numerous complaints and convictions for implant ruptures filed in Great Britain and France in particular, how could the national and European agencies have missed such a swindle?
This tragedy immediately led us to consider improving the European system for controlling medical devices, as you have just explained.
Commissioner, we must now build a new system capable of avoiding scandal, but it is a new system that must be built.
As you said, this new legislation is due in the autumn.
As a co-author of this resolution, I am obviously satisfied.
But, Commissioner, you seem to want to take into account a large part of our concerns, as set out in this resolution.
So why make an exception that is unacceptable to many MEPs here present?
Why not want the creation of a marketing authorisation for Class III medical devices, the highest-risk ones?
Commissioner Dalli, can you explain your position?
Mr President, we are currently experiencing a situation where production is increasing by more than 2.5%. At the same time, industry is reducing prices.
In Italy, in Germany, in England and in all European countries, prices are falling.
This clearly means that prices are linked to production volumes, and we need to organise both production volumes and prices at the same time.
At the same time, today producers are suffering from an increase of more than 44% in the price of vegetable proteins and 30% in the price of cereals.
Parliament has already voted in favour of ensuring that added value is shared between producers and that farmers are not exploited by companies and large retailers.

So I am asking the Commission today, but also the Council, to reconsider the challenge to production control. At the same time, I am saying very clearly that the Member States must clean up their own act, because there are countries that have respected the quota rules and others that have not.
That is also what led to these quotas being called into question.
There is therefore a double responsibility: that of the Commission and that of the Member States.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, a single European railway area, now that would be a fine policy of European integration.
This is a fine policy, in which European integration is at the heart of the daily concerns of Europeans.

Too often criticised for being broken, Europe finally, through this directive, shows the way for a major policy on sustainable transport and opens the field for a major policy on rail investment for employment.

I welcome the main measures taken in the organisation of the railway network, the setting up of national regulators in each Member State, the creation of a five-year multiannual investment contract stipulating the funding commitments of the

rail infrastructure, but also, I will limit myself to this, the establishment of transparency of financial flows between the managers and the operators within the framework of integrated companies. The call for non-discriminatory access to certain

services. Finally, I welcome the confirmation sought by the MEPs of certain guarantees, particularly on the social front, for example, the absence of any reference to minimum services, as well as the total separation between the CENR and the operator.

But let me raise a requirement: we must stop thinking about, particularly on the part of the European Commission, the liberalisation of the rail sector as the only strategic goal for the development of a single rail area, and instead decide on a European investment strategy that is integrated and favourable to employment in Europe.
Finally, I would like to ask you a question, Commissioner Kallas.
three days after the European Council of 28 and 29 June, after the validation of a 120 billion euro growth pact to relaunch the European economy.
particularly through investment in infrastructure. Commissioner, could you tell us what amount the European rail sector is likely to benefit from under this growth package?
Madam President, we will have a debate tomorrow on ACTA, but I would like to raise a question tonight which is certainly peripheral, but extremely important in this context.
These were the words expressed by Commissioner De Gucht in the International Trade Committee on Wednesday 20 June.
I quote: 'If you decide on a negative vote before the European Court rules, let me tell you that the Commission will continue to pursue the current procedure before the Court of Justice.
We have committed ourselves to that.
A negative vote will not stop the procedure before the Court of Justice.

Justice. Not only do these remarks seem to ignore the Treaties, but, above all, and this is what I want to denounce, they show an unacceptable contempt for the European Parliament, for democracy in general and for the citizens whom we represent.
At a time when European citizens, as a whole,
Mr President, a lot has been said at this hour.
Mr President, Commissioner, I would like to thank you very warmly.
Thanks to your obstinacy and your procedural tricks, you are enabling us to prove the importance of Parliament as a co-legislator.
Thank you, Commissioner, for having encouraged hundreds of thousands of citizens to take to the streets and for having encouraged 2.8 million to sign petitions.
They discovered that democracy is played out here, that we Members of the European Parliament are the representatives of the people, their representatives.
Thank you again to the millions of Internet users who followed the debates and votes in committee and who are still following us today.
I welcome them: they are a breath of fresh air in our institutions, which are all too often seen as technocratic.
Thank you, Commissioner, but in a democracy, there is another principle.
This is the fundamental principle, the principle of responsibility.
Tomorrow, we will take our responsibility, and I would like to thank you tomorrow for also taking your responsibility, your failure, the failure of your working method.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I am speaking on behalf of Maurice Ponga, the rapporteur for the opinion of the Committee on Development, on the Gallagher report.
It seemed important to me to point out that, when it wants to sanction a country that practices unsustainable fishing, the Union must take account of its level of development.

Indeed, in the name of the principle of policy coherence with development objectives, it is crucial, in my opinion, that the measures adopted in fisheries should not undermine the development plans and strategies put in place by the European Union in these countries.
Mr Gallagher's draft report takes up this important idea for our development committee by providing that the measures should not be unjustified or even discriminatory.

I therefore invite the European Commission, when adopting such measures, to respect this principle of policy coherence with development objectives, and to duly take into account the level of development and the vulnerability of countries.
The third reform of the common fisheries policy must not once again be carried out against the interests of fishermen.

No, not all fishermen are these plunderers of the sea, solely responsible for the poor state of fish stocks, as environmental organisations and the European Commission claim, lumping together all categories of fishing.
Of course, we must preserve fish stocks, but we must also allow our fishermen to live from their activity and preserve the economic fabric, dynamism and cultural identity of our coastal regions.
Yet the main measures of the European executive will, once again, reduce the number of fishermen in order to achieve the mythical maximum sustainable yield,
I would remind you that the French fleet has decreased by more than 50% since 1980.

The Commission proposes, in particular, the introduction of individual transferable quotas, which will lead to industrialisation and concentration in the sector, or the total ban on discards, which professionals consider unrealistic.
This reform project is probably suitable for the industrial fleet, but as it stands, it would mean the scheduled death of what remains of local and artisanal fishing.

Mr President, Commissioner, I would like to congratulate the rapporteur, Mr Karas, our colleague from the EPP, on this difficult report, given the sensitive nature of bilateral negotiations in the field of energy between European countries and third countries.
The agreement reached in trialogue will allow the Commission proposal to be implemented quickly and, out of pragmatism, we can only welcome that.
Europe is making progress in the energy field, certainly not fast enough, but it is making progress.
As a major energy importer, the European Union is structurally dependent on certain supplier countries.

In order to have a more European reflection on our energy strategy, it became necessary to disseminate information on energy agreements more widely to the European Commission, while respecting the principle of subsidiarity.
The establishment of an information mechanism on intergovernmental agreements will therefore improve transparency on the energy market and thus make it possible to anticipate energy supply problems.
I am pleased that the present decision does not create any obligations in relation to agreements between commercial companies.
In times of crisis and given the stakes in terms of competition and competitiveness, we had to protect the interests of our companies and the rules for protecting private contracts.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to thank our two rapporteurs for the work they have done on this file.

It was necessary, because we were starting from a very distant position with the Commission's basic proposal: no specific mention of the outermost regions, directly affected by competition from Latin American countries, no automatic triggering

of the stabilization mechanism, no possibility offered to our Assembly to request the opening of a safeguard investigation, no consideration of social and environmental standards as constituting an unfair competitive advantage.
That is why it is extremely important that tomorrow's vote validates the progress made on these loans in the Committee on International Trade.
Nevertheless, this is not enough, because we know that the triggering thresholds of these two regulations are so high that they make these mechanisms practically inapplicable.
Moreover, the Commission must provide additional financial resources to support the competitiveness of Community banana production.
Let us bear in mind that many jobs are at stake in these European regions, which already have record unemployment rates.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, I would like to thank Mr Goerens for his excellent report.

Mr President, as we all know, the European Union provides more than half of the world ’ s total public development aid, and yet, as the deadline for the MDGs approaches in 2015, even if some objectives seem
we must ask ourselves how we can improve our aid and make it more coherent with our trade policy, with our agricultural policy and with our foreign policy.
The future of the European development policy must be based on precise, measurable commitments, and according to a short- and medium-term schedule.

The economic crisis, climate change, the power of emerging countries and land grabbing are forcing us to define new priorities: priorities to ensure food security, access to energy resources and access to healthcare.

Certainly, a European consensus exists, but we must rethink European and international institutional systems in environmental and social matters, taking inspiration from bodies that work well in decision-making terms, such as the WTO, for example.
Finally and above all, we must explain to our fellow citizens that the development of third countries is not just charity and solidarity, but also a source of development for us.
We need only look at the markets opening up for Europeans in the emerging countries, China, Brazil and India.
2015 will also be an opportunity to take better account of the geostrategic changes of recent years, as today's emerging countries were only recently developing countries.
Now, as major economic powers, these countries must also contribute to international aid.
The European Union must promote responsible development aid at European and international level.

2015 will be more than a deadline; it will be a unique opportunity to rethink development aid, not as simple financial assistance, but as support for responsible development projects, both socially and environmentally.

I therefore join my colleagues from the other groups and from the Economic and Social Committee and the Committee of the Regions in calling for 2015 to be designated the European Year of Development and, above all, of responsible development.
Mr President, the Commission ’ s agenda for change raises many questions in my opinion, the main one being the practical implementation of the principle of differentiation.

The Commission would like that, from 2014, the choice of countries in which the European Union will invest its development aid will be based on the mechanical application of the simple criterion of wealth per inhabitant.
As Mrs. Ashton reminded us, these issues are not only accounting matters, but deserve a real political debate on the definition of our priorities in international cooperation.
Moreover, this differentiated approach is not, in my opinion, in line with the spirit of the Cotonou Agreement.

I am convinced that the ACP countries that have achieved positive economic performance should be encouraged in their transition towards more sustainable growth. These countries should not be unduly penalized for having succeeded in guaranteeing growth through discipline, good governance and prudent economic policies.
Madam President, Commissioner, we welcome the initiative taken by Jacques Delors twenty years ago, at the very time of the fall of the Berlin Wall.
Despite all these advances that we have seen since the beginning of our debate, we must admit that the single act is today synonymous with injustice, inequality and economic liberalism.
I would therefore like to hear from you, Mr Barnier, Commissioner, to tell us how we are going to build together a fairer Europe, a facilitating Europe, a protective Europe.

There are a number of avenues, and I would like to hear your views on these avenues, which are at the heart of the economic and social crisis as we unfortunately know it: fiscal harmonisation, public services.
protective, a minimum wage in Europe, the implementation of enhanced cooperation that creates jobs, facilitating cross-border cooperation and developing a fair-trade policy.
These are the issues, I believe, that we face twenty years on.
I am counting on you, Commissioner, to help us carry forward this wonderful policy of European integration.

Mr President, as a member of the Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection, I have followed closely the report by my colleague, Mrs Bastos, on the 20 main concerns of citizens and businesses.
We have debated this report, in connection with our debate on the 20 years of the internal market.
It has been twenty years now that the free movement of goods, people, services and capital is supposed to be able to operate fully within our area of life, trade and freedom, the internal market.

I was particularly interested in the fact that among the top concerns expressed by our European constituents is the lack of professional qualifications, and that we really need to be particularly proactive and particularly effective.

This is what our fellow citizens expect: to be able to put in place, in the very near future, procedures for recognition of their diplomas, their skills and their professional qualifications that are really faster, simpler, more effective and less costly.
This is a real challenge.

As shadow rapporteur for the EPP Group on this subject, the modernisation of these procedures, I am actively working with the other members of the IMCO Committee to truly meet this major challenge of mobility.
More mobility for more Europe: that is what our fellow citizens are asking for.
Mr President, this year, the Committee on Petitions has acquired the right to be automatically associated with hearings on European citizens ’ initiatives.
This is, for me, the strong signal of 2011 for this Commission.
Citizens already had a Commission to turn to in the event of a breach of their rights, but they will also now be able to turn to it when they have proposals for improving European legislation.
I hope that the Committee on Petitions can thus increasingly be seen as the European Parliament ’ s main interlocutor for citizens.
This problem of consideration can also be found in the results of the opinions of the Committee on Petitions.
Too often, we are approached by citizens who are facing legal entanglements on which we would like to take a position. But while the problems are European,
solutions should be European, we remain powerless, because subsidiarity does not always go hand in hand with European solutions due to a lack of national will.
I will take the difficult example of cross-border divorces. The problems are both national and European when custody or visitation rights cross national borders, in this case, those of Germany.
Despite the obvious difficulties for many parents in seeing their children born of binational unions, we remain powerless to act in Parliament.

My proposal that all European citizens should have the right to a national ombudsman, especially from Germany, which sends an enormous number of petitions, on the model of the French 'Défenseur des droits', has had no effect.
Yet in the case of cross-border divorces, this remains a solution that reconciles European problems and national solutions.
In many cases, European law seems powerless to meet citizens ’ expectations.
The European Year of Citizens 2013 should deal with citizens' complaints, listen to them, but we need the means to act in order to be credible.
